title: ALU implementation: rotate/shift (CB prefix)
description:
Implement CB-prefix rotate and shift operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- RLC r: rotate left circular, C=old bit 7, Z=result==0
- RRC r: rotate right circular, C=old bit 0, Z=result==0
- RL r: rotate left through carry, C=old bit 7, bit 0=old C, Z=result==0
- RR r: rotate right through carry, C=old bit 0, bit 7=old C, Z=result==0
- SLA r: shift left arithmetic, C=old bit 7, bit 0=0, Z=result==0
- SRA r: shift right arithmetic, C=old bit 0, bit 7 preserved, Z=result==0
- SRL r: shift right logical, C=old bit 0, bit 7=0, Z=result==0
- SWAP r: swap nibbles, Z=result==0, all other flags 0

Also: unprefixed rotate/shift (RLCA, RRCA, RLA, RRA):
- Same as RLC A, RRC A, RL A, RR A BUT Z flag is always 0 (not set on zero result!)
- This difference between prefixed and unprefixed rotates is a common bug.

title: SM83 assembler toolchain and ROM build pipeline
description:
Set up the SM83 assembler toolchain for building custom test ROMs.

TOOLS NEEDED:
- rgbds (rgbasm, rgblink, rgbfix): the standard Game Boy assembler/linker/fixer
- Pin exact version in toolchain/versions.md
- Install via devenv, cx, or nix (per CLAUDE.md conventions)

ROM BUILD PIPELINE:
- Create bench/roms/ directory for ROM source (.asm) and built artifacts (.gb, .sym, .map)
- Create bench/roms/Makefile (or shell script) that:
  1. Assembles each .asm → .o with rgbasm
  2. Links .o → .gb + .sym + .map with rgblink
  3. Fixes header with rgbfix
  4. Validates .sym file has required labels (__pass, __fail)
- Each ROM source file follows the checkpoint ABI (§17.0.1)

ROM SOURCE TEMPLATE:
- Create bench/roms/template.inc with:
  - Standard header (Nintendo logo, title, checksum)
  - SkipBoot entry point at 0x0100
  - __pass and __fail label stubs
  - WRAM signature block macros
  - Helper macros for test assertions (compare register, compare memory, report pass/fail)
  - Detailed per-test logging: each test case writes test ID + expected + actual to WRAM before checking

LOGGING CONTRACT:
- Every test case in every ROM writes to a WRAM log region:
  - 2-byte test ID (unique within ROM)
  - 1-byte status (0x00=not run, 0x01=pass, 0xFF=fail)
  - N bytes of diagnostic data (expected value, actual value, relevant flags)
- The harness reads this log region after ROM execution to produce detailed per-test-case reports
- On failure, the log tells you WHICH test case failed and WHY, not just 'ROM failed'

VERIFICATION:
- Build a trivial 'hello' ROM that writes a known value to WRAM and hits __pass
- Verify the .sym file is generated and contains __pass/__fail
- Verify rgbfix produces a valid Game Boy ROM header

WHY THIS IS CRITICAL: Every 'Green X ROM' bead requires building a custom assembly ROM. Without this toolchain, none of those beads can start.

title: Green MBC3_SWITCH custom ROM
description:
Write and green MBC3_SWITCH.

Scope:
- ROM bank switching only
- Verify 7-bit bank selection and bank 0→1 remap

Why split: basic MBC3 ROM banking can go green before RAM/RTC behavior.

title: E2E milestone gate test: Milestone D -- interrupt/timer ready
description:
Create tools/gate_milestone_d.sh -- acceptance gate for Milestone D (§22: Interrupt/Timer Ready).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (§22 Milestone D):
1. EI_DELAY and TIMER_IRQ_HALT ROMs green
2. Live instruction-commit differential green on ALL Wave B ROMs
3. Strict M-cycle differential green on targeted subsets (selected timer/interrupt sequences)
4. Seeded interrupt injection scenarios green (bd-27r)
5. Every interrupt source observed in at least one green test: timer, serial (stub), joypad, VBlank (stub)
6. HALT wake behavior confirmed: HALT with pending IE&IF wakes correctly
7. EI+HALT and interrupt edge case interaction tests pass (bd-1g0)
8. JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST passes with deterministic joypad injection

SCRIPT VALIDATES:
- All Wave B ROM tests pass
- JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST passes
- Interrupt injection scenario tests pass
- EI+HALT edge case tests pass
- Timer unit tests pass (bd-v2p)
- Interrupt controller unit tests pass (bd-11x)
- Coverage report confirms all interrupt sources exercised

LOGGING: Per-test pass/fail with interrupt source coverage matrix.

ACCEPTANCE: Script exits 0 when all criteria pass. Milestone D confirms the CPU handles all time-dependent and asynchronous behavior correctly.

title: Interrupt controller unit tests (IE/IF, priority, acknowledge)
description:
Create test/unit/test_interrupts.py — isolated tests for IE/IF storage, pending-vector computation, priority selection, and acknowledge behavior.

These tests should drive the peripheral-side acknowledge input directly and intentionally do NOT wait for the full CPU ServiceInterrupt sequencer. The point is to validate interrupt-controller semantics independently before higher-level interrupt sequencing is debugged.

TEST CASES:
1. IE register read/write and upper-bit behavior
2. IF register read/write, including externally asserted interrupt sources
3. irq_pending = IE & IF across the full 5-bit space
4. Priority resolution when multiple sources are pending
5. Acknowledge clears only the selected IF bit
6. Edge cases around simultaneous set/clear activity

LOGGING: print IE/IF/pending state transitions so failures localize to the controller itself, not the CPU phase machine.

title: Green MBC1_SWITCH custom ROM
description:
Write and green MBC1_SWITCH.

Scope:
- ROM bank switching only
- Verify bank 0→1 remap and 0x20/0x40/0x60 special-case wrapping

Why split: ROM banking can go green before RAM banking.

title: ALU unit tests green (curated + generated vectors)
description:
Green test/unit/test_alu_generated_vectors.py and test/unit/test_alu.py.

Three verification lanes (§11.4):
1. Curated examples: hand-picked edge cases testing specific flag behaviors
   - ADD 0xFF + 0x01 = 0x00 (Z=1, C=1, H=1)
   - ADC 0x0F + 0x00 + carry=1 (H=1 from carry into high nibble)
   - SUB 0x00 - 0x01 (borrow, Z=0, N=1, H=1, C=1)
   - INC 0xFF = 0x00 (Z=1, C unchanged!)
   - DAA after 0x09 + 0x01 = 0x10 (BCD correction)
   
2. Generated vectors: systematic sweep from gbxcule-derived reference
   - Every ADD A,x for x in representative set
   - Every ADC with carry=0 and carry=1
   - ADD SP,e8 for representative SP values and signed offsets
   - LD HL,SP+e8 with same cases

3. Nightly exhaustive (pytest marker for CI):
   - All ADD A,x for all x (256*256 = 65536 cases)
   - All ADC A,x with carry 0/1 (256*256*2 = 131072 cases)
   - All SUB x, all SBC with carry 0/1
   - All DAA inputs over (A:256, N:2, H:2, C:2) = 2048 cases

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone B): All ALU unit tests green.

title: Misc single-cycle CPU instructions: NOP, STOP stub, DI, EI basic handling
description:
Implement phase machine handling for miscellaneous single-cycle instructions that don't fit the load/ALU/flow/CB families.

INSTRUCTIONS:
- NOP (0x00): single fetch cycle, no state change at all. The simplest possible instruction.
- DI (0xF3): set ime_state = Disabled. Takes effect immediately.
- EI (0xFB): set ime_state = PendingEnable. Full PendingEnable→Enabled transition comes in Phase 4 (T53/bd-3o3). For Phase 3b, PendingEnable is set but never transitions to Enabled (no interrupt check in fetch yet). This is correct because without an interrupt system, the IME value has no observable effect.
- STOP (0x10): stub implementation — behaves like a 2-byte NOP for now. Real STOP puts the CPU in low-power mode and has complex behavior involving the joypad register. Full implementation deferred.
- DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF (0x27, 0x2F, 0x37, 0x3F): these are already handled by the ALU phase machine (T42/bd-3ab), but verify they appear in the decoder and route correctly.

WHY THIS IS CRITICAL:
Every custom test ROM will contain NOP instructions (for alignment, timing, or between test cases). Most ROMs start with DI to disable interrupts during setup. Without handling these opcodes, the CPU will enter an undefined state on the very first NOP in any test ROM, making ALL Wave A ROM beads impossible to green.

The Phase 3b phase machine beads (T41-T45) deliberately organize by instruction FAMILY:
- T41: loads
- T42: 8-bit ALU
- T43: 16-bit arithmetic
- T44: stack/control flow
- T45: CB-prefix
None of these cover NOP, DI, EI, or STOP. This bead fills that gap.

VERIFICATION:
- Add test cases to test/unit/test_misc_instructions.py:
  - NOP: verify no registers change, PC increments by 1, 1 M-cycle
  - DI: verify ime_state = Disabled, 1 M-cycle
  - EI: verify ime_state = PendingEnable, 1 M-cycle
  - STOP: verify PC increments by 2 (2-byte instruction), 1 M-cycle
- Each test uses detailed logging: log instruction, expected state, actual state, diff

title: ALU implementation: INC/DEC 8-bit
description:
Implement 8-bit INC/DEC in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- INC r: r + 1, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=half-carry, C=PRESERVED (not affected!)
- DEC r: r - 1, flags Z=result==0, N=1, H=half-borrow, C=PRESERVED

TRICKY: INC/DEC PRESERVE the carry flag (§11.3). This is a common bug source. The f_prev input carries the previous flags, and only Z/N/H are updated.

title: Epic: CPU Core Bring-Up — Wave A (Phase 3b)
description:
Implement the CPU phase machine and bring all Wave A custom ROMs to green at instruction-boundary differential against the PyBoy oracle.

Wave A ROMs (§17.1):
- LOADS_BASIC: 8-bit loads, 16-bit loads, memory reads/writes via register pairs
- ALU_FLAGS: all 8-bit ALU ops with flag verification
- ALU16_SP: 16-bit arithmetic, ADD SP,e8, LD HL,SP+e8
- FLOW_STACK: PUSH, POP, CALL, RET, JP, JR (conditional and unconditional)
- CB_BITOPS: all CB-prefix rotate/shift/bit/res/set operations
- MEM_RWB: memory read/write-back soak test
- ALU_LOOP: ALU stress/iteration test

Implementation scope:
- Fetch/execute phase machine using the Phase enum from §8.1
- Typed continuation-driven control flow (§8.2)
- cpu_core entity wrapping step_mcycle pure function (§7.3)
- Minimal bus fabric: ROM + WRAM + HRAM + dummy IO reads (Wave A from §12.4)
- Write-enable discipline (§9.4): registers update only on explicit writeback
- Formal checks: F low nibble invariant, m_ce=0 hold behavior
- Metamorphic tests (§16.5): untouched registers unchanged, branch-not-taken uses short cycle

This phase does NOT include timers, interrupts, DMA, MBC, or PPU.

RATIONALE: Wave A deliberately covers most of the CPU instruction set without requiring any peripherals. This is the wave-based staging strategy (§17, Risk 5 mitigation) that prevents peripheral work from obscuring CPU bugs. The cpu_core entity is NOT a pipeline (§4.2)—it's a state machine with explicit registers. The phase machine uses typed continuations to make illegal control states unrepresentable.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone C): All Wave A ROMs green at instruction-boundary differential. No unexplained PC/register divergence. F low nibble invariant green.

title: Green ALU_LOOP stress ROM
description:
Write the ALU_LOOP stress test ROM and green it.

Purpose: extended ALU computation loops to stress the CPU over many instruction cycles.

Test approach:
- Fibonacci-style computation loop with register accumulation
- CRC-like computation using XOR/shift chains
- Counter loops with conditional branches
- Verify final register state against known values

This exercises the CPU over thousands of instructions, catching subtle state corruption that single-instruction tests miss.

Oracle mode: checkpoint (compare at loop endpoints, not every instruction)

title: Epic: Test Harness Infrastructure (Phase 1)
description:
Build the entire verification harness before writing real HDL. This is the plan's most counterintuitive but most important sequencing decision.

Covers:
- PyBoy oracle interface (§14.1): reset, step_commit, read_mem, write_event, snapshot, restore
- Hook-driven adapter for strict lockstep (§14.3): symbol lookup, instruction-boundary hooks, M-cycle stepping
- Replay capsule format (§16.7): ROM id, model/reset profile, seed, event log, symbols hash, first-divergence metadata
- Deterministic perturbation engine (§16.5.1): seeded event scripts keyed by commit index, not wall-clock time
- Simulation-only stimulus ports (§12.6: SimStimulus record)
- Divergence artifact framework: first bad CommitTrace, commit window, minimized replay capsule, localized waveform
- Scaffolded test files: decode completeness, ALU generated vectors, reset profiles, event determinism
- Placeholder lockstep test as xfail(strict=True)

RATIONALE: Thesis #2 says harness first. The plan explicitly warns (§16.1) against 'everything fails' and instead prescribes sanity-green meta tests proving generators/fixtures work, plus red semantic tests proving HDL isn't correct yet. Building the harness first means that when HDL lands in Phase 2, tests immediately fail on VALUE mismatches, not infrastructure errors. This is the difference between a harness that catches bugs and one that hides them.

The oracle is PyBoy-only (thesis #6). No second CPU emulator. No file-based golden traces (thesis #7). The replay capsule format provides reproducibility without trace files—it records the minimal information needed to re-derive any execution.

ACCEPTANCE (Milestone A): Generators run reproducibly. Smoke scripts work from clean checkout. Harness tests green. HDL tests fail semantically, not infrastructurally.

title: Green MBC1_SWITCH and MBC1_RAM custom ROMs
description:
Write and green MBC1_SWITCH and MBC1_RAM test ROMs.

MBC1_SWITCH:
- Switch between ROM banks and read expected data
- Verify bank 0→1 mapping
- Verify bank wrapping for banks 0x00, 0x20, 0x40, 0x60
- Verify upper bits work in ROM banking mode

MBC1_RAM:
- Enable external RAM
- Write and read back data across RAM banks
- Verify RAM bank switching in RAM mode
- Verify RAM is inaccessible when disabled (reads return 0xFF)

Oracle mode: instr_commit
close_reason: Superseded by separate MBC1_SWITCH and MBC1_RAM beads

title: CommitTrace and DebugTrace type definitions
description:
Create src/cpu/debug.spade with trace types from §7.2.

CommitTrace — the versioned comparison boundary (simulation-only):
  enum CommitKind { MCycle, InstrCommit, InterruptAck, Checkpoint }
  struct CommitTrace {
    schema_version: uint<16>,
    kind: CommitKind,
    seq: uint<64>,
    pc_before: uint<16>,
    opcode: Option<uint<8>>,
    arch_after: CpuArchState,
    phase_after: Phase,
    bus_req: BusReq,
    bus_resp: BusResp,
    mem_touch: Option<MemTouch>,
    io_touch: Option<IoTouch>,
  }
  struct MemTouch { kind: MemTouchKind, addr: uint<16>, data: uint<8> }
  struct IoTouch { addr: uint<16>, data: uint<8> }

DebugTrace — narrow internal debug record:
  (Define a minimal internal debug record for hardware-optional debug)

Compare scopes (§7.3):
- Every commit: PC/SP/registers/IME/halt/phase/bus req/bus resp
- On memory touch: address/data/region
- At checkpoints: selected memory windows or signatures
- PPU tests: semantic objects first, full frame hash last

DESIGN CONSTRAINTS (§4.4):
- CommitTrace is simulation-only, exposed only through simulation/test tops
- DebugTrace is narrow, internal only
- Hardware tops are FREE of trace fanout
- Schema version allows CommitTrace format evolution without breaking replay capsules

title: Bus ownership arbitration (BusOwner)
description:
Implement bus ownership arbitration in src/bus/membus.spade.

BusOwner enum (§12.2.2):
  enum BusOwner { Cpu, OamDma, Ppu, Idle }

Arbitration rules:
- Default owner: Cpu
- During OAM DMA: OamDma (CPU restricted to HRAM)
- During PPU pixel transfer: Ppu owns VRAM (CPU VRAM reads return 0xFF)
- During PPU OAM search: Ppu owns OAM (CPU OAM reads return 0xFF)

The CPU always emits plain BusReq. Contention is resolved in the fabric:
- If CPU requests a region owned by another master, the fabric returns 0xFF (DmgConservative) and the write is ignored
- BusObs sideband records the blocked access for diagnostics

This is separate from the CPU because bus ownership is a system-level concern (§12.2.2).

title: Serial port stub (SB/SC registers)
description:
Implement minimal serial port registers in src/periph/serial.spade.

The serial port is needed for Wave C (§12.4) and is also commonly used by test ROMs for debug output (writing to SB with SC=0x81 sends a byte to the "serial console").

REGISTERS:
- SB (0xFF01): Serial transfer data. Read/write. Holds the byte being transferred.
- SC (0xFF02): Serial transfer control.
  - Bit 7: Transfer start flag (1=start, cleared when transfer complete)
  - Bit 1: Clock speed (CGB only, ignore for DMG)
  - Bit 0: Clock select (0=external clock, 1=internal clock)

MINIMAL IMPLEMENTATION (Wave C scope):
- SB: simple read/write register
- SC: when written with bit 7=1 and bit 0=1 (internal clock):
  - After 8 serial clock cycles, transfer completes
  - SB receives 0xFF (no connected device)
  - SC bit 7 cleared
  - IF bit 3 (Serial) set
- External clock mode: no action (no external device connected)

FOR TEST ROM DEBUG OUTPUT:
- Many test ROMs use serial output to log results
- The harness should capture bytes written to SB when SC=0x81
- bench/pyboy/serial_capture.py: collects serial output into a string for test assertions

TESTING:
- test/unit/test_serial.py:
  1. SB read/write
  2. Internal clock transfer completes after correct cycle count
  3. Transfer sets IF serial bit
  4. Transfer result is 0xFF (no peer)
  5. External clock mode does nothing

WHY SERIAL MATTERS: Without serial, test ROMs that use serial debug output will trigger unexpected interrupts or hang waiting for serial transfer completion. This stub prevents those failure modes.

title: Oracle comparison framework: CommitTrace vs PyBoy differential
description:
Implement the Python comparison logic that bridges DUT CommitTrace output with PyBoy oracle state, producing detailed differential reports on mismatch.

This is the CORE VERIFICATION LOOP that all ROM tests and lockstep tests use.

FILES:
- bench/pyboy/comparator.py: CommitTrace comparison engine
  - compare_commit(dut_trace: CommitTrace, oracle_state: OracleCommit, scope: CompareScope) -> CompareResult
  - CompareResult: match/mismatch, field-by-field diffs, first divergent field
  - CompareScope (from spec/compare_scopes.py): which fields to compare at each granularity

- bench/pyboy/lockstep_driver.py: Lockstep test orchestrator
  - load_rom(rom_path, sym_path, manifest_entry)
  - run_lockstep(oracle, dut_driver, event_script, oracle_mode) -> TestResult
  - The main loop:
    1. Step DUT one M-cycle (or instruction boundary, per oracle_mode)
    2. Step oracle to matching boundary
    3. Extract DUT CommitTrace from simulation ports
    4. Extract oracle state via Oracle.step_commit()
    5. Compare using comparator with appropriate scope
    6. On mismatch: emit divergence artifacts (bd-2wl) and stop
    7. On match: log progress and continue
  - Support for checkpoint symbols: if __checkpoint_N reached, do extended comparison

- bench/pyboy/trace_formatter.py: Human-readable trace output
  - Format CommitTrace as: "M#1234 PC=0x0150 OP=0x3E(LD A,n) A=0x42 F=0x80 BC=0x0000 ..."
  - On mismatch, show side-by-side:
    "DUT:    A=0x42 F=0x80 [Z=1 N=0 H=0 C=0]"
    "Oracle: A=0x42 F=0xB0 [Z=1 N=0 H=1 C=1]"
    "DIFF:            ^^^^       ^^^   ^^^"
  - Color-coded output for terminal

RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER BEADS:
- Uses: bd-f10 (oracle interface), spec/compare_scopes.py (bd-3hr)
- Used by: bd-3gv (lockstep test), bd-27r (interrupt injection), ALL "Green X ROM" beads
- Divergence artifacts (bd-2wl) are invoked by this framework on mismatch

WHY THIS IS ITS OWN BEAD: The oracle interface (bd-f10) defines HOW to talk to PyBoy. This bead defines HOW TO COMPARE DUT output against oracle output. The comparison logic (scope selection, field-by-field diff, trace formatting) is substantial and reusable.

title: Replay capsule format definition
description:
Define the replay capsule format (§16.7) in bench/pyboy/replay.py.

A replay capsule is the minimal information needed to reproduce any test execution:
- ROM id (from manifest)
- ModelProfile
- ResetProfile
- MemoryBehaviorProfile
- Random seed (for generated action sequences)
- Injected event log (list of (commit_index, SimEvent) pairs)
- Symbol/hook manifest identity (hash of .sym file + hook config)
- First-divergence metadata (commit index, field, expected vs actual)

The capsule provides reproducibility WITHOUT file-based golden traces (thesis #7). Any engineer can re-derive the full execution from the capsule by:
1. Loading the ROM
2. Configuring the profiles
3. Replaying the event log with the same seed
4. Running to the divergence point

Capsules should be serializable to JSON for human readability and YAML for manifest integration.

title: Waveform capture and debug visualization configuration
description:
Configure VCD/FST waveform capture for all Cocotb tests and create helper scripts for debugging failed tests.

PURPOSE: When a test fails, the developer needs to inspect signal-level behavior. Waveform files (VCD or FST) are the primary debugging tool for HDL. This bead ensures every test CAN produce waveforms and provides tools to make them useful.

CONFIGURATION:
- test/harness/waveform_config.py: Waveform capture control
  - By default: waveform capture OFF (tests run faster)
  - --waves flag enables capture for specific tests
  - On test FAILURE: automatically save waveform for the failed test
  - Configurable signal groups: cpu_core internals, bus activity, timer, interrupts
  - Waveform output: bench/artifacts/waves/<test_name>.vcd

HELPER SCRIPTS:
- tools/view_waves.sh <test_name>: Opens waveform in GTKWave with pre-configured signal layout
- tools/gtkwave_layouts/: GTKWave save files (.gtkw) with organized signal groups:
  - cpu_overview.gtkw: PC, SP, registers, flags, phase, m_ce
  - bus_activity.gtkw: bus_req, bus_resp, address, data, bus_owner
  - interrupt_flow.gtkw: IE, IF, IME, irq_pending, irq_ack, halt_state
  - timer_detail.gtkw: sys_counter, DIV, TIMA, TMA, TAC, timer_irq
  - power_signals.gtkw: write-enables, alu_req (idle vs active), bus_req (idle vs active)

DIVERGENCE WINDOW CAPTURE:
- When oracle comparison detects a mismatch (via bd-2wl divergence artifacts):
  - Save a LOCALIZED waveform: 50 M-cycles before and 10 M-cycles after the divergence point
  - Include all CPU-internal signals in the localized capture
  - Print the waveform file path in the test failure output

TESTING:
- Verify VCD file is produced when --waves is enabled
- Verify VCD file is NOT produced when --waves is disabled (performance)
- Verify localized divergence waveform captures the correct time window

title: Green ALU16_SP custom ROM
description:
Write the ALU16_SP custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- ADD HL,BC/DE/HL/SP with half-carry and carry edge cases
- INC/DEC rr (no flag changes)
- ADD SP,e8 with positive and negative offsets, verifying low-byte flag behavior
- LD HL,SP+e8 with same cases

Critical edge cases:
- ADD SP,1 with SP=0x00FF (half-carry from bit 3, carry from bit 7 of LOW byte)
- ADD SP,-1 with SP=0x0000
- ADD HL,rr where bit 11 carry occurs
- INC/DEC rr across 0x0000/0xFFFF boundary (no flags affected)

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Green EI_DELAY custom ROM
description:
Write and green the EI_DELAY test ROM.

Test coverage:
- EI followed by NOP: interrupts enabled after NOP, not during
- EI followed by HALT: interrupts enabled, then halt waits
- EI followed by DI: DI takes effect, canceling the pending enable
- Multiple EI instructions: still only delayed by one instruction
- EI followed by an instruction that should complete before interrupt service

Verification approach:
- Set up timer to fire interrupt at known time
- Execute EI at precise timing relative to pending interrupt
- Verify interrupt is NOT serviced during the instruction immediately after EI
- Verify interrupt IS serviced starting from the second instruction after EI

This tests the ImeState::PendingEnable → Enabled transition timing exactly.

Oracle mode: mcycle_commit (timing must be exact)

title: EI+HALT and interrupt edge case interaction tests
description:
Create test/lockstep/test_ei_halt_corners.py — targeted tests for the most subtle interrupt/HALT interactions.

These are the cases that break almost every Game Boy emulator at least once. The plan (§16.5) explicitly calls out "halt-after-ei corner cases" and "deterministic interrupt injection around EI, HALT, RETI, and pending-IF cases."

TEST CASES:

1. EI → HALT (the canonical pattern):
   - EI sets IME=PendingEnable
   - HALT is the next instruction
   - Question: does HALT execute with IME=PendingEnable or IME=Enabled?
   - Answer: HALT executes with PendingEnable. The PendingEnable→Enabled transition happens AFTER HALT starts. So HALT enters halted state, THEN IME becomes Enabled, THEN interrupt wakes and services.
   - Verify: interrupt IS serviced (not just a wake without service)

2. EI → NOP → HALT:
   - EI sets PendingEnable
   - NOP executes: PendingEnable → Enabled
   - HALT with IME=Enabled: behaves normally
   - Verify timing matches case 1

3. DI → HALT with pending interrupt:
   - DI sets IME=Disabled
   - IF bit already set, IE matches
   - HALT wakes immediately (because IE & IF != 0) but does NOT service interrupt
   - CPU resumes at PC after HALT, still with IME=Disabled
   - Verify: no interrupt service occurs

4. EI → interrupt fires before HALT:
   - EI sets PendingEnable
   - An interrupt becomes pending during the next instruction fetch
   - The EI delay means PendingEnable→Enabled happens after the next instruction STARTS
   - If the next instruction is interruptible (single-M-cycle), does the interrupt fire?
   - Answer: No — IME transitions at the END of the next instruction, then the interrupt check happens on the FOLLOWING fetch.

5. RETI → immediate interrupt:
   - RETI sets IME=Enabled immediately (unlike EI)
   - If an interrupt is already pending, it should fire on the NEXT instruction fetch
   - Verify: interrupt fires immediately after RETI returns

6. Nested interrupts:
   - Interrupt handler uses EI+HALT to wait for next interrupt
   - Verify: nested interrupt service works correctly

7. IF bit set during interrupt service:
   - While servicing interrupt A, interrupt B becomes pending
   - Verify: interrupt B is serviced after RETI from handler A

LOGGING: Each test logs cycle-by-cycle IME state and interrupt activity:
  [M#100] EI executed → IME=PendingEnable
  [M#101] HALT entered → HaltState=Halted, IME still PendingEnable
  [M#102] IME transition → IME=Enabled (HALT still active)
  [M#105] IF[2] set (timer) → IE&IF=0b00100 → wake!
  [M#106] ServiceInterrupt begins → IME=Disabled, push PC...

All stimuli injected via deterministic perturbation engine (bd-35l) keyed by commit index.

title: Verilator backend configuration for long regressions
description:
Configure Verilator as an alternative Cocotb simulator backend for long-running regression suites (§16.4).

RATIONALE: Icarus Verilog is the default simulator for development because it compiles fast and has broad SystemVerilog support. However, for nightly exhaustive ALU sweeps, full Wave A-D ROM regressions, and M-cycle lockstep over thousands of instructions, Icarus simulation speed becomes the bottleneck. Verilator compiles Verilog to optimized C++ and provides 10-100x simulation speedup for long runs.

The plan (§16.4) explicitly states: 'If more speed is required for long regressions, use Verilator as the Cocotb simulator backend. Do not make a separate C++/Verilator authored-test lane part of the core bring-up plan.' This means the SAME Python/Cocotb tests run on both backends--no separate C++ test code.

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Pin Verilator version in toolchain/versions.md alongside Icarus
- Configure cocotb Makefile/runner to accept SIM=icarus (default) or SIM=verilator
- Verify all Cocotb test fixtures work with both backends
- Add tools/run_tests.py --sim=verilator option
- Document any Verilator-specific limitations (signal visibility, VPI differences)

PERFORMANCE TARGETS:
- Nightly exhaustive ALU sweeps (bd-3ud): < 10 minutes with Verilator vs potentially hours with Icarus
- Full Wave A ROM regression: < 5 minutes with Verilator

WHY NOT VERILATOR BY DEFAULT: Verilator's compilation step is slow for small tests and its VPI support has edge cases. Icarus gives faster edit-compile-test cycles for development. Verilator is for regressions, not iteration.

ACCEPTANCE: All existing Cocotb tests pass with SIM=verilator. Nightly suite completes within target time.

title: OAM DMA controller
description:
Implement OAM DMA controller in src/periph/ or src/bus/.

DMA behavior:
- Writing to 0xFF46 starts DMA: copies 160 bytes from source (written_value * 0x100) to OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F)
- Transfer takes 160 M-cycles
- During DMA, CPU can only access HRAM (0xFF80-0xFFFE) and IO registers
- All other memory reads return 0xFF (or bus conflict value per MemoryBehaviorProfile)

Implementation:
- DMA state machine entity with source address counter
- BusOwner::OamDma takes bus ownership during transfer
- Bus fabric routes DMA reads to source, DMA writes to OAM
- CPU reads during DMA are restricted (handled in bus fabric, NOT in CPU)

The CPU doesn't need to know about DMA restrictions — the bus fabric handles it (§12.2.2).

title: Cocotb test base infrastructure and shared utilities
description:
Implement test/harness/ shared Cocotb infrastructure used by ALL dynamic verification tests.

CORE COMPONENTS:

1. test/harness/dut_driver.py: DUT interaction layer
   - CpuTestDriver: wraps cpu_test_top Cocotb DUT
     - reset(reset_profile): apply SkipBoot or RawPowerOn reset sequence
     - step_mcycle(): advance DUT by one M-cycle, return CommitTrace
     - step_instruction(): advance DUT until next instruction boundary
     - read_signal(name): read named signal from DUT
     - inject_stimulus(stim: SimStimulus): apply simulation sideband
   - SoCLockstepDriver: wraps soc_lockstep_top for full-SoC tests

2. test/harness/fixtures.py: Shared pytest/Cocotb fixtures
   - cpu_dut: provides initialized CpuTestDriver
   - oracle: provides initialized PyBoy Oracle
   - lockstep_pair: provides paired (dut, oracle) for differential testing
   - rom_loader(manifest_entry): loads ROM into DUT memory and oracle
   - event_script(seed, manifest_entry): generates deterministic event sequence

3. test/harness/assertions.py: Comparison utilities
   - assert_registers_match(dut_state, oracle_state, scope)
   - assert_commit_trace_match(dut_trace, oracle_commit, scope)
   - format_register_diff(expected, actual): human-readable diff

4. test/harness/logging_std.py: Test logging standard (per bd-dny)
   - TestLogger class with hierarchical output
   - Automatic failure context collection

DESIGN PRINCIPLES:
- All DUT interaction goes through the driver layer (never raw signal access in tests)
- Fixtures handle setup/teardown lifecycle
- Assertions produce actionable diagnostics on failure
- The same infrastructure supports unit tests, ROM tests, and lockstep tests

ACCEPTANCE: At least one test (bd-1jm Spade-Cocotb smoke) uses the infrastructure. All fixtures documented. Driver API stable enough for Phase 3+ tests.

title: MBC3 mapper (ROM/RAM bank switching + RTC)
description:
Implement MBC3 memory bank controller.

MBC3 registers:
- 0x0000-0x1FFF: RAM/RTC Enable
- 0x2000-0x3FFF: ROM Bank Number (7 bits, 0 maps to 1)
- 0x4000-0x5FFF: RAM Bank / RTC Register Select (0x00-0x03: RAM, 0x08-0x0C: RTC)
- 0x6000-0x7FFF: Latch Clock Data (write 0x00 then 0x01 to latch)

RTC registers (optional, can stub initially):
- 0x08: Seconds, 0x09: Minutes, 0x0A: Hours, 0x0B: Day low, 0x0C: Day high + flags

Address mapping:
- 0x0000-0x3FFF: ROM bank 0 (always)
- 0x4000-0x7FFF: ROM bank N (7-bit, 1-127)
- 0xA000-0xBFFF: RAM bank or RTC register

For initial bring-up, RTC can return fixed values. Focus on ROM/RAM banking.

title: Phase machine for stack and control flow (PUSH, POP, JP, JR, CALL, RET, RST)
description:
Implement phase machine for stack operations and control flow.

Stack operations:
- PUSH rr: 4 M-cycles (fetch + internal + write high + write low). SP decremented first.
- POP rr: 3 M-cycles (fetch + read low + read high). SP incremented after each read.
  POP AF: low nibble of F is masked to zero (§9.3).

Unconditional jumps:
- JP nn: 4 M-cycles (fetch + read low + read high + internal)
- JP (HL): 1 M-cycle (fetch only, PC ← HL)
- JR e8: 3 M-cycles (fetch + read offset + internal branch)

Conditional jumps (cc = NZ, Z, NC, C):
- JP cc,nn: 3 M-cycles if not taken, 4 if taken
- JR cc,e8: 2 M-cycles if not taken, 3 if taken

Subroutine calls:
- CALL nn: 6 M-cycles (fetch + read low + read high + internal + push high PC + push low PC)
- CALL cc,nn: 3 if not taken, 6 if taken
- RST vec: 4 M-cycles (fetch + internal + push high + push low). Target = vec*8.

Returns:
- RET: 4 M-cycles (fetch + pop low + pop high + internal)
- RET cc: 2 if not taken, 5 if taken (extra internal cycle for condition check)
- RETI: like RET but also sets IME=Enabled

METAMORPHIC TEST (§16.5): Branch not taken MUST use the short cycle tuple.

title: Spade-to-Cocotb integration smoke test
description:
Create test/harness/test_spade_cocotb_integration.py — validates the entire toolchain pipeline end-to-end.

This is a 'sanity-green meta test' (§16.1) that proves the toolchain works, not that any CPU logic is correct.

PIPELINE UNDER TEST:
  Spade source (.spade) → swim compile → Verilog (.v) → simulator (iverilog/verilator) → Cocotb testbench

TEST STEPS:
1. Use the existing main.spade (or a trivial test module) as the Spade source
2. Compile it with swim to produce Verilog
3. Load the Verilog into the simulator
4. Drive the module from a Cocotb testbench:
   - Assert reset
   - Release reset
   - Clock for N cycles
   - Read output signals and verify they match expected behavior
5. Verify waveform dump (VCD) is produced and non-empty

DETAILED LOGGING:
- Log each pipeline step with timestamps and exit codes
- On failure, print the exact command that failed, its stderr, and the state of intermediate files
- On success, print summary: 'Spade compile OK (0.3s) → Verilog OK (42 lines) → Simulator OK → Cocotb 1/1 pass'

WHY THIS IS ITS OWN BEAD:
T03 (Cocotb pin) mentions 'verify Cocotb can drive a trivial Spade-generated Verilog testbench' in passing. But this validation is critical infrastructure — if this pipeline doesn't work, NO Cocotb test can run. It deserves focused attention, explicit pass criteria, and a reusable test that can catch regressions if swim/cocotb/simulator versions change.

title: Sprite rendering
description:
Implement sprite (OBJ) rendering.

OAM scan (Mode 2):
- Scan all 40 OAM entries
- Select up to 10 sprites visible on current scanline
- Sprite visible if: Y-16 <= LY < Y-16+height (height = 8 or 16 per LCDC bit 2)

Sprite attributes (4 bytes per entry):
- Byte 0: Y position (on-screen Y = stored_Y - 16)
- Byte 1: X position (on-screen X = stored_X - 8)
- Byte 2: Tile index (for 8x16 mode, bit 0 ignored)
- Byte 3: Flags
  - Bit 7: BG priority (0=sprite above BG, 1=sprite behind non-zero BG)
  - Bit 6: Y flip
  - Bit 5: X flip
  - Bit 4: Palette (0=OBP0, 1=OBP1)

Rendering priority:
- Sprites with lower X position have higher priority
- If same X, lower OAM index has higher priority
- Color 0 in sprite is always transparent

8x16 mode: tile index bit 0 forced to 0, top tile = index, bottom tile = index+1.

title: Constrained synth_ice40 + nextpnr baseline
description:
Run initial synthesis and place-and-route to establish baselines (§18.7 tier 3).

Steps:
1. Run synth_ice40 with explicit clock constraints
2. Run nextpnr-ice40 with the icebreaker.pcf pin file
3. Record baseline metrics:
   - LUT count
   - DFF count
   - SPRAM usage (4 blocks available on UP5K)
   - EBR usage (30 blocks available on UP5K)
   - Estimated f_max under clock constraint
   - Clock-enable fanout
4. Verify no debug-path contamination: hardware build should NOT include CommitTrace/DebugTrace fanout

The hardware synthesis top (src/board/icebreaker_top.spade) must be clean of simulation-only signals (§18.6).

Record results in a trackable format (JSON or markdown table) for regression comparison.

title: Opcode family coverage tracking infrastructure
description:
Build infrastructure to automatically track which opcode families, phase/continuation constructors, bus regions, interrupt causes, ROM suites, and reset/model profiles are covered by the test suite (§16.3).

RATIONALE: The plan requires tracking coverage across six dimensions. Without automated tracking, coverage gaps are invisible until they cause bugs in production. The canonical opcode metadata source (bd-36u) provides the ground truth for what SHOULD be covered; this task builds the system that measures what IS covered.

COVERAGE DIMENSIONS:
1. Opcode families: group by mnemonic (LD, ADD, SUB, etc.) -- which families have >=1 test?
2. Phase/continuation constructors: which Phase enum variants and ReadCont/WriteCont variants are exercised?
3. Bus regions: which BusRegion values are exercised (Rom, Wram, Hram, Io, Vram, Oam, etc.)?
4. Interrupt causes: which interrupt sources (timer, serial, joypad, VBlank, STAT) are exercised?
5. ROM suites: which custom ROMs from the manifest have passing tests?
6. Reset/model profiles: which (ModelProfile, ResetProfile, MemoryBehaviorProfile) combos are tested?

IMPLEMENTATION:
- test/harness/coverage_tracker.py:
  - Collects coverage events during test execution (e.g., "opcode 0x80 executed", "BusRegion::Wram accessed")
  - Persists to bench/artifacts/coverage/coverage.json after each test run
  - Generates coverage report showing gaps per dimension

- Integration with test runner (bd-2wk):
  - --coverage flag enables coverage collection
  - Summary shows coverage percentages per dimension
  - CI can fail if coverage drops below threshold

- Integration with opcode metadata (bd-36u):
  - cross-references exercised opcodes against full opcode table
  - reports untested opcodes and untested flag policies

COVERAGE REPORT FORMAT:
  === Opcode Family Coverage ===
  LD: 23/28 variants tested (82%)
  ADD: 8/8 variants tested (100%)
  SUB: 4/8 variants tested (50%)  <-- GAP
  ...
  === Phase Constructor Coverage ===
  Fetch: covered
  ReadImm8: covered
  ReadImm16Lo: covered
  WriteMem: NOT COVERED  <-- GAP
  ...

ACCEPTANCE: Coverage report generated after full test suite run. All dimensions tracked. Coverage gaps clearly identified.

title: test_reset_profile.py scaffold
description:
Create test/harness/test_reset_profile.py (Phase 1 step 9).

This test verifies that the DUT's initial state after reset matches the expected post-boot state for the configured (ModelProfile, ResetProfile) pair.

For DMG + SkipBoot, verify:
- A=0x01, F=0xB0, B=0x00, C=0x13, D=0x00, E=0xD8, H=0x01, L=0x4D
- SP=0xFFFE, PC=0x0100
- IE=0x00, IF=0xE1 (typical)
- IME=disabled
- Timer registers at expected values
- Key IO registers at expected values

The test should:
1. Reset both DUT and oracle with the same profile
2. Compare initial register state
3. Optionally compare key memory locations

This is a 'sanity-green meta test' (§16.1) — it proves the reset infrastructure works, not that the CPU executes correctly.

title: cpu_test_top simulation top
description:
Create src/sim/cpu_test_top.spade — isolated CPU testing simulation top.

This top provides:
- Instantiates cpu_core with controllable inputs
- Mock/minimal bus: responds to reads from a small memory, accepts writes
- Controllable m_ce input for test-driven stepping
- Controllable irq_pending for interrupt injection
- Exposes CommitTrace outputs as flat Verilog-friendly ports for Cocotb
- SimStimulus input ports for deterministic test stimuli

Port naming: use stable, Verilog-friendly names (§4.4). Cocotb tests will reference these by name.

This is SEPARATE from the hardware synthesis top (§4.4, §18.6). Debug/trace fanout exists here, never in icebreaker_top.

title: test_alu_generated_vectors.py scaffold
description:
Create test/unit/test_alu_generated_vectors.py (§16.2, Phase 1 step 7).

This test generates ALU input/output vectors from a Python reference model (spec/flag_policies.py) and verifies the Spade ALU against them.

Three verification lanes (§11.4):
1. Curated examples: hand-picked edge cases for readability (inline in test)
2. Generated vectors: systematic sweep from gbxcule-derived flag formulas
3. Nightly exhaustive: selected small-domain full sweeps (behind pytest marker)

Initial scaffold should:
1. Define the Python reference ALU (spec/flag_policies.py)
2. Generate vectors for at least: ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, CP, INC, DEC
3. Parameterize tests with generated vectors
4. FAIL until ALU is implemented (red semantic tests)

Tricky flag cases to cover from §11.3:
- ADC/SBC: half-carry computed on nibble+carry, not nibble alone
- INC/DEC: preserves carry flag
- ADD SP,e8: zero/half-carry from low byte, not high byte
- LD HL,SP+e8: same flag behavior as ADD SP,e8
- DAA: depends on N, H, C flags AND accumulator value
- SCF/CCF: clear N and H, SCF sets carry, CCF inverts carry

title: cpu_core entity stub
description:
Create the cpu_core entity STUB in src/cpu/core.spade (Phase 2, step 17).

This is a compile-only stub that defines the entity signature and returns dummy values. It does NOT contain working logic--that comes in Phase 3b when the step_mcycle framework (bd-2wu) and phase machines are implemented.

ENTITY SIGNATURE (from plan section 6.2):
  entity cpu_core(
      clk: clock,
      rst: bool,
      m_ce: bool,
      bus_resp: BusResp,
      irq_pending: IrqPending,
  ) -> (BusReq, CommitTrace)

STUB BEHAVIOR:
- On reset: initialize all registers to SkipBoot values:
  A=0x01, F=0xB0, B=0x00, C=0x13, D=0x00, E=0xD8, H=0x01, L=0x4D, SP=0xFFFE, PC=0x0100
  IME=Disabled, HALT=Running, Phase=Fetch
- On m_ce=true: return BusReq::Idle and a dummy CommitTrace (wrong values, tests should fail semantically)
- On m_ce=false: hold all state

ARCHITECTURAL CONSTRAINTS (enforced from day one):
- Uses reg(clk) for ALL state with explicit hold behavior
- State changes ONLY when m_ce is true
- Returns sparse CpuDelta, not full-state rewrite (critical for power)
- No pipeline abstraction--this is an entity with explicit registers

WHY A STUB: Phase 2 locks type-level API contracts. The stub lets Phase 1 harness tests execute against real compiled Verilog and fail on VALUE mismatches instead of build errors.

TRANSITION TO REAL: The step_mcycle framework task (bd-2wu) fills in the real logic. Each phase machine task adds match arms. The stub evolves into the real cpu_core incrementally.

ACCEPTANCE: Entity compiles. Tests can instantiate it. Reset produces correct initial state. m_ce=false holds state. m_ce=true produces (wrong but non-crashing) output.

title: Create repository directory structure per §5 layout
description:
Create the full directory tree specified in §5 of the architecture plan. This is the physical organization that enforces the denotational/operational split.

Directories to create:
- spec/           — machine-readable opcode metadata, flag policies, compare scopes (Python)
- formal/         — SymbiYosys .sby files for formal verification
- tools/          — local smoke/regress/power/formal shell scripts
- toolchain/      — version pins and lockfiles for all external tools
- bench/manifests/ — ROM manifest YAML files
- bench/pyboy/    — oracle adapter: oracle.py, hooks.py, symbols.py, hook_driver.py, replay.py, checkpoints.py
- bench/actions/  — test action generators
- src/cpu/        — CPU semantic and operational code
- src/bus/        — bus types, memory fabric, address decode
- src/mem/logical/ — logical memory map
- src/mem/phys/   — physical memory primitives (EBR, SPRAM, inferred)
- src/periph/     — timer, serial, interrupts
- src/sim/        — simulation-only tops (cpu_test_top, soc_lockstep_top, inject_top, trace_top)
- src/board/      — hardware synthesis tops (clockgen, icebreaker_top)
- test/harness/   — harness infrastructure tests
- test/unit/      — isolated Spade block unit tests
- test/lockstep/  — live oracle lockstep tests
- test/rom/       — ROM-based integration tests
- test/ppu/       — PPU-specific tests
- test/power/     — power/activity tests

Move existing main.spade content: icebreaker_top goes to src/board/icebreaker_top.spade, update swim.toml source paths.

IMPORTANT: The sim/ vs board/ split is load-bearing for power (§18.6). Debug/trace fanout must NEVER be in hardware tops by default.

title: Green TIMER_DIV_BASIC custom ROM
description:
Write and green the TIMER_DIV_BASIC test ROM.

Test coverage:
- DIV increments at correct rate (every 256 T-cycles = 64 M-cycles for DMG)
- Writing to DIV resets the internal 16-bit counter to 0
- TIMA increments at rate selected by TAC clock select
- TAC enable/disable controls TIMA counting
- TIMA overflow triggers reload from TMA
- TIMA overflow sets IF bit 2
- Timer reload timing (1 M-cycle delay after overflow)

Edge cases:
- DIV write causing TIMA increment (falling-edge detection)
- TAC clock select change causing spurious increment
- Writing TIMA during the reload delay cycle cancels reload

Oracle mode: instr_commit or mcycle_commit for timing-critical tests

title: PPU mode state machine unit tests
description:
Create test/unit/test_ppu_modes.py — isolated tests for PPU mode transitions and timing.

PPU MODES:
- Mode 0 (HBlank): ~204 dots, CPU can access VRAM/OAM
- Mode 1 (VBlank): 10 scanlines (4560 dots), CPU can access VRAM/OAM
- Mode 2 (OAM Search): 80 dots, CPU cannot access OAM
- Mode 3 (Pixel Transfer): 168-291 dots (variable), CPU cannot access VRAM or OAM

MODE TRANSITIONS PER SCANLINE (lines 0-143):
  Mode 2 (80 dots) → Mode 3 (variable) → Mode 0 (remainder of 456 dots)
VBLANK (lines 144-153):
  Mode 1 for all 10 scanlines

TEST CASES:
1. Basic mode sequence: verify Mode 2→3→0 for one scanline, then VBlank after line 143
2. LY counter: verify LY increments each scanline, resets to 0 after line 153
3. LYC coincidence: verify STAT LYC=LY flag sets when LY matches LYC register
4. STAT interrupt sources:
   - Mode 0 (HBlank) interrupt
   - Mode 1 (VBlank) interrupt  
   - Mode 2 (OAM) interrupt
   - LYC=LY coincidence interrupt
5. STAT register bits: read-only mode bits, writable interrupt enable bits
6. Mode timing precision: verify exact dot counts for each mode transition
7. LCDC bit 7 (LCD enable): when cleared, PPU is off, LY=0, mode=0
8. LCDC enable/disable timing: verify clean transition

LOGGING: Each test logs mode transitions with dot/scanline precision:
  [LINE 0] Mode 2 @ dot 0 → Mode 3 @ dot 80 → Mode 0 @ dot 252 
  [LINE 1] Mode 2 @ dot 456 → Mode 3 @ dot 536 → Mode 0 @ dot 708
  ...
  [LINE 144] Mode 1 @ dot 65664

title: Epic: PPU Integration — Wave D (Phase 4.6)
description:
Implement the Picture Processing Unit (PPU) and bring Wave D ROMs to green.

Wave D0 — PPU timing/interrupt (§17.4):
- PPU mode state machine (OAM search → pixel transfer → HBlank → VBlank)
- STAT interrupt generation (mode transitions, LY=LYC coincidence)
- LCDC, STAT, LY, LYC, SCX, SCY, WX, WY, BGP, OBP0, OBP1 registers

Wave D1 — render semantics/visuals (§17.4):
- Background rendering: tilemap fetch, tile data lookup, palette application
- Scrolling: SCX/SCY with signed wrap
- Window rendering: WX/WY positioning
- Sprite rendering: OAM scan, priority, flip, palette

Memory regions:
- VRAM (0x8000-0x9FFF): tile data + tilemaps
- OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F): sprite attribute table

Verification approach (§17.4):
- Semantic checks: tilemap/window/sprite state queries, NOT just frame hashes
- PPU_STAT_IRQ: STAT interrupt timing and mode transitions
- BG_STATIC: static background rendering
- BG_SCROLL_SIGNED: scrolled background with signed offset arithmetic
- PPU_WINDOW: window overlay rendering
- PPU_SPRITES: sprite rendering with priority/flip/palette

RATIONALE: PPU is deliberately last in the CPU-first plan. It has the most complex timing interactions and the largest state space. By the time we reach Wave D, the CPU, timer, interrupt, and bus infrastructure are all verified, so PPU bugs can be isolated to PPU logic rather than being masked by CPU/timer issues (Risk 5 mitigation).

ACCEPTANCE: All Wave D ROMs green. PPU mode timing matches oracle. Semantic sprite/tile checks pass before frame hash verification.

title: Interrupt injection test scenarios with detailed logging
description:
Create comprehensive, deterministic interrupt injection test scenarios (§16.5) with detailed per-cycle logging for debugging interrupt timing issues.

This task COMPLEMENTS bd-27r (Green deterministic interrupt injection scenarios) by providing the detailed TEST SPECIFICATIONS and logging format. bd-27r validates that injection scenarios pass; this task defines WHICH scenarios and HOW they log.

SCENARIOS (test/lockstep/test_interrupt_injection.py):

1. BASIC INTERRUPT DISPATCH:
   - Inject timer interrupt (IF bit 2) while CPU is running
   - Verify: CPU pushes PC, jumps to vector 0x0050, IF bit cleared
   - Log: each M-cycle of the interrupt service sequence

2. EI DELAY PRECISION:
   - Execute: EI / NOP / <next instruction>
   - Inject interrupt during EI, verify NOT serviced until after NOP
   - Log: IME state at each instruction boundary

3. HALT WAKE VARIANTS:
   a. HALT with IME=1: wake + service interrupt
   b. HALT with IME=0, IE&IF!=0: wake WITHOUT servicing (HALT bug path)
   c. HALT with IME=0, IE&IF==0: stay halted indefinitely
   - Log: halt entry/exit cycle, bus activity during halt

4. INTERRUPT PRIORITY:
   - Set multiple IF bits simultaneously (e.g., VBlank + Timer)
   - Verify: highest priority (lowest bit) serviced first
   - Log: which interrupt acknowledged, in which order

5. DI/EI INTERACTION:
   - DI during interrupt service, verify subsequent interrupts blocked
   - RETI, verify interrupts re-enabled
   - EI immediately followed by DI, verify interrupts stay disabled

6. INTERRUPT DURING MULTI-CYCLE INSTRUCTION:
   - Inject interrupt while a 3-M-cycle instruction is executing
   - Verify: interrupt is serviced AFTER instruction completes, not mid-instruction
   - Log: instruction completion M-cycle vs interrupt service M-cycle

7. TIMER OVERFLOW TIMING:
   - Configure timer for known overflow point
   - Verify: IF bit set on exact expected M-cycle
   - Log: TIMA value at each M-cycle leading up to overflow

8. NESTED INTERRUPT EDGE CASE:
   - Service interrupt A, enable interrupts mid-ISR (EI), trigger interrupt B
   - Verify: B serviced, B returns to A's ISR, A returns to main code
   - Log: full call/return stack trace

LOGGING FORMAT (per scenario):
  [SCENARIO] halt_wake_ime_enabled
    [SETUP] IE=0x04 (timer), IME=Enabled
    [INJECT] IF bit 2 at commit #47
    [CYCLE 47] Phase=Halted, bus=Idle, IME=Enabled
    [CYCLE 48] Phase=Halted, bus=Idle, wake detected (IE & IF = 0x04)
    [CYCLE 49] Phase=ServiceInterrupt{irq=Timer, subphase=PushPCHi}
    [CYCLE 50] Phase=ServiceInterrupt{irq=Timer, subphase=PushPCLo}
    [CYCLE 51] Phase=ServiceInterrupt{irq=Timer, subphase=JumpToVector}
    [CYCLE 52] Phase=Fetch, PC=0x0050, IF bit 2 cleared
    [PASS] halt_wake_ime_enabled

ACCEPTANCE: All 8 scenario categories pass. Logging output matches expected format. Each scenario exercises a distinct interrupt timing edge case.

title: Regression test suite tier definitions and nightly configuration
description:
Define the three regression test tiers and configure the nightly exhaustive suite.

TIER DEFINITIONS:

1. SMOKE (< 30 seconds):
   - Spade compilation check (swim build)
   - Cocotb infrastructure smoke (bd-1jm)
   - Oracle init/reset check
   - E2E smoke test (bd-2up)
   Run: on every commit, before any other work

2. FULL (< 10 minutes):
   - All unit tests (ALU, decoder, register, timer, bus, interrupts)
   - All formal checks (F low nibble, reset state, m_ce hold)
   - All Wave A-D ROM differential tests
   - Metamorphic and invariant tests
   - Lockstep tests (instruction-boundary granularity)
   Run: after completing each bead, before closing

3. NIGHTLY (< 2 hours):
   - Everything in FULL
   - ALU exhaustive sweeps (bd-3ud): all ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/DAA inputs
   - M-cycle-granularity lockstep on targeted subsets
   - Extended ROM execution (10000+ commits per ROM)
   - Power/activity capture (SAIF, write-enable duty cycle)
   - Synthesis + P&R resource/timing check
   Run: nightly via cron or manual trigger

CONFIGURATION FILES:
- test/tiers.yaml: maps test files to tiers, with expected-fail entries
- tools/run_tests.py --tier smoke|full|nightly: run specific tier
- bench/artifacts/: all test outputs stored here with timestamps

XFAIL MANAGEMENT (§16.1):
- xfail entries MUST have a reason and a linked bead ID
- xfail(strict=True): test must FAIL; if it passes, that is also a failure (catches premature greening)
- No blanket xfails — each failing test is individually tracked
- When a bead is closed, its xfails must be removed or the test promoted to expected-pass

COVERAGE REPORT:
- After each FULL run, print coverage summary:
  Opcode families: 85% (34/40 families with ≥1 passing test)
  Phase constructors: 70% (14/20 Phase variants exercised)
  Bus regions: 60% (6/10 regions accessed)
  Interrupt sources: 40% (2/5 sources tested)
  ROM suites: 7/15 green

NIGHTLY ALERT:
- If any previously-green test goes RED in nightly, emit a high-priority alert
- Include the exact commit that broke it (binary search via git bisect if needed)

title: HALT implementation
description:
Implement HALT behavior in the CPU phase machine.

States (§8.3):
  enum HaltState {
    Running,          // Normal execution
    Halted,           // CPU halted, waiting for interrupt
    HaltBugPending,   // HALT bug: next byte read twice (defer implementation)
  }

HALT behavior:
- When HALT executed: transition to HaltState::Halted
- In Halted state: CPU emits BusReq::Idle, no register writes, no ALU activity (§18.5)
- Wake condition: (IE & IF) != 0 (any enabled interrupt is pending)
- If IME=Enabled AND wake: service interrupt (transition to ServiceInterrupt phase)
- If IME=Disabled AND wake: resume execution at PC (no interrupt service)

HALT bug (defer to later):
- If IME=Disabled AND (IE & IF)==0 when HALT executes: HALT bug triggered
- Next instruction's first byte is read twice (PC doesn't increment on first read)
- HaltBugPending type exists now but implementation can be stubbed

POWER (§18.5): HALT must mean REAL stillness:
- No register writes
- No bus requests
- No ALU activity
- Stable phase/debug outputs in simulation

This is both a correctness requirement and a power architecture requirement.

title: Epic: HDL Type System & Skeleton (Phase 2)
description:
Create the compile-only Spade HDL skeleton: all types, stubs, and simulation tops that define the semantic boundaries of the CPU.

This phase produces NO working logic—only type definitions and stub entities that compile cleanly. The purpose is to:
1. Lock the type-level API contracts before implementation
2. Let Phase 1 tests run against stubs and fail on value mismatches, not build errors
3. Establish the denotational/operational split (§4.1) in code structure

Key type boundaries from §7.2:
- CpuArchState (regs, ime_state, halt_state)
- CpuMicroState (phase, opcode_latch, imm_lo/hi, decoded, temp)
- Phase enum with typed continuations (§8.1-8.2)
- CommitTrace with schema versioning (§7.2)
- BusReq/BusResp (§12.2) — CPU-facing, minimal
- BusObs/BusOwner (§12.2.1-12.2.2) — fabric-side, simulation-only
- AluReq with Idle case for operand isolation (§11.1-11.2)
- CpuDelta with sparse register writes (§7.2) — critical for power (§18.4)

Simulation tops:
- cpu_test_top: isolated CPU testing with controllable bus/interrupts
- soc_lockstep_top: full SoC for lockstep oracle comparison

RATIONALE: The Conal-style split (§4.1) is the architectural heart. Pure functions specify combinational semantics; entities own state. The type system encodes this split. Typed continuations (§8.2) make illegal phase transitions unrepresentable. The CpuDelta sparse-write pattern (§18.4) is designed-in from day one because power is an architectural property (thesis #3), not a cleanup pass.

ACCEPTANCE: All .spade files compile. Tests execute and fail on value mismatches, not build errors.

title: Formal verification: m_ce=0 architectural state hold
description:
Create formal/cpu_hold.sby — proves that when m_ce=0, ALL architectural state is frozen.

PROPERTY TO VERIFY:
For ANY state the CPU can be in, if m_ce is held low for N cycles:
- All registers (A, F, B, C, D, E, H, L, SP, PC) are unchanged
- IME state is unchanged
- HALT state is unchanged
- Phase is unchanged
- No bus requests are emitted (BusReq remains whatever it was)
- No register write-enables are asserted

This is the FOUNDATIONAL power property (§6.2, §18.1): architectural state does not toggle at board-clock rate. The entire power architecture rests on this invariant.

WHY FORMAL: This property must hold for EVERY possible CPU state, not just states we think to test. A simulation test checks a finite number of states; formal proves the property universally. If this property is violated, the power architecture is broken.

TOOL: yosys + sby (SymbiYosys). Use k-induction to prove the invariant holds at any depth.

RELATIONSHIP TO bd-838 (metamorphic tests): bd-838 tests m_ce=0 hold via simulation across sampled states. This bead proves it formally for ALL states. Both should exist.

title: Physical memory mapping: SPRAM/EBR for large memories
description:
Verify and implement proper physical memory mapping (§18.7.1).

UP5K resources:
- 4 x SPRAM blocks (256Kbit each = 32KB each, single-port)
- 30 x EBR blocks (4Kbit each = 512B each, true dual-port)

Memory mapping plan:
- WRAM (8KB): 1 SPRAM block (uses 8KB of 32KB capacity) or 16 EBR blocks
- VRAM (8KB): 1 SPRAM block
- OAM (160 bytes): inferred registers or 1 EBR block
- HRAM (127 bytes): inferred registers
- Cartridge RAM: SPRAM or external

Implementation:
- src/mem/phys/spram.spade: SPRAM primitive wrapper
- src/mem/phys/ebr.spade: EBR primitive wrapper
- src/mem/logical/map.spade: logical-to-physical mapping

CRITICAL: Do not let large memories remain accidental register arrays in hardware builds. The clean wrapper boundary (§18.7.1) allows physical mapping changes without affecting CPU/bus semantics.

title: HALT quiescence verification test
description:
Create test/power/test_halt_quiescence.py using the shared harness on cpu_test_top.

This is a Phase 5 power-validation bead even though it consumes HALT behavior implemented in Wave B. Its job is to prove that HALT is not just functionally correct, but actually quiescent.

Verify HALT means real stillness (§18.5):
1. Enter HALT state
2. Run for many M-cycles with no wake event
3. Verify on every cycle:
   - No register writes
   - BusReq stays Idle
   - AluReq stays Idle
   - Phase output is stable
   - Debug/trace outputs are stable
4. Trigger a wake event and verify the wake cycle and subsequent interrupt behavior are correct

This test is part of the first power baseline, not just the interrupt milestone.

title: Echo RAM, unusable region, and memory behavior profile enforcement
description:
Implement the remaining memory map edge cases and enforce the MemoryBehaviorProfile (§13.1.1).

ECHO RAM (0xE000-0xFDFF):
- Mirrors WRAM (0xC000-0xDDFF). Reads/writes to Echo RAM map to WRAM.
- Echo RAM is 8KB but only mirrors the first 7.5KB (0xE000-0xFDFF = 0x1E00 bytes)
- Implementation: in the bus address decoder, map 0xE000-0xFDFF to 0xC000-0xDDFF

UNUSABLE REGION (0xFEA0-0xFEFF):
- Behavior depends on hardware revision and DMA state
- DmgConservative profile: reads return 0xFF, writes are ignored (safest default)
- DmgRevisionSpecific profile: varies — some revisions return ANDed OAM data
- For CPU bring-up, pin DmgConservative (§13.1.1)

BLOCKED VRAM/OAM ACCESS:
- During PPU pixel transfer (mode 3): VRAM reads return 0xFF, writes are ignored
- During OAM search (mode 2) and pixel transfer (mode 3): OAM reads return 0xFF, writes are ignored
- During OAM DMA: CPU cannot access most of memory (only HRAM accessible)
- These restrictions are enforced in the memory fabric, NOT in the CPU (§12.2.2)

MEMORY BEHAVIOR PROFILE ENFORCEMENT:
- MemoryBehaviorProfile enum (from bd-331) selects which set of restrictions apply
- The bus fabric must consult the active profile when resolving access to edge-case regions
- Record the active profile in replay capsules for reproducibility

TESTING (test/unit/test_memory_map.py):
1. Echo RAM: write to 0xC000, read from 0xE000, verify match (and vice versa)
2. Echo RAM boundary: verify 0xFE00+ is NOT echo RAM (it is OAM)
3. Unusable region: read from 0xFEA0, verify 0xFF under DmgConservative
4. Blocked VRAM: set PPU to mode 3, attempt VRAM read, verify 0xFF
5. Blocked OAM: set PPU to mode 2, attempt OAM read, verify 0xFF
6. DMA restriction: during OAM DMA, verify only HRAM is accessible
7. Log each test with address, expected value, actual value, active profile

NOTE: VRAM/OAM blocking tests (#4-6) require PPU mode state or DMA state, so those specific sub-tests are gated on Phase 4.5/4.6 completion. Echo RAM and unusable region tests can run as soon as the bus fabric exists.

title: Pin PyBoy dependency with headless configuration
description:
Add PyBoy as a pinned Python dependency for the oracle/verification layer.

Requirements:
- Pin exact PyBoy version in toolchain/python.lock or requirements.txt
- Configure for headless mode: null window plugin, sound emulation off, unlimited speed (§14.2)
- Verify PyBoy can load a test ROM, step instructions, read registers, read/write memory
- Verify save/load checkpoint (snapshot/restore) works
- Document the specific PyBoy APIs we depend on in toolchain/versions.md

PyBoy is the SOLE behavioral oracle (thesis #6). We do NOT add a second CPU emulator. The hook-driven adapter (separate task) provides the deep control path for lockstep testing.

Run PyBoy in headless performance mode for all non-visual suites: null window, no input, sound emulation off, unlimited speed (§14.2).

title: Phase machine for CB-prefix operations
description:
Implement phase machine for CB-prefixed instructions.

CB prefix handling:
- Fetch 0xCB → Phase::FetchPrefix{prefix: Cb} → fetch next byte → decode_cb → execute

For register operands (B,C,D,E,H,L,A):
- 2 M-cycles: fetch CB + fetch opcode → execute ALU

For (HL) operand:
- BIT b,(HL): 3 M-cycles (fetch CB + fetch opcode + read (HL))
- RES/SET b,(HL): 4 M-cycles (fetch CB + fetch opcode + read (HL) + write (HL))
- Rotate/shift (HL): 4 M-cycles (fetch CB + fetch opcode + read (HL) + write (HL))

The continuation for CB (HL) operations is a ReadMem with the appropriate continuation for the CB operation type.

title: test_event_script_determinism.py scaffold
description:
Create test/harness/test_event_script_determinism.py (Phase 1 step 10).

This test verifies that the deterministic perturbation engine produces identical results across runs:
1. Generate an event script with seed S
2. Run the oracle with that script for N commits
3. Record the final state
4. Repeat with the same seed S
5. Assert identical final state

Also verify:
- Different seeds produce different event scripts
- Event scripts are keyed by commit index, not wall-clock time
- The same event stream applied to both DUT and oracle produces deterministic comparison results

This is another 'sanity-green meta test' (§16.1) — it validates the test infrastructure itself.

title: PPU registers (LCDC, SCX, SCY, WX, WY, BGP, OBP)
description:
Implement PPU control and palette registers.

LCDC (0xFF40) — LCD Control:
- Bit 7: LCD enable
- Bit 6: Window tilemap select (0=9800, 1=9C00)
- Bit 5: Window enable
- Bit 4: BG & Window tile data select (0=8800, 1=8000)
- Bit 3: BG tilemap select (0=9800, 1=9C00)
- Bit 2: OBJ size (0=8x8, 1=8x16)
- Bit 1: OBJ enable
- Bit 0: BG & Window enable/priority

Scroll registers:
- SCX (0xFF43): viewport X scroll
- SCY (0xFF42): viewport Y scroll
- WX (0xFF4B): window X position + 7
- WY (0xFF4A): window Y position

Palette registers:
- BGP (0xFF47): BG palette data (4 x 2-bit color indices)
- OBP0 (0xFF48): OBJ palette 0 (color 0 is transparent)
- OBP1 (0xFF49): OBJ palette 1 (color 0 is transparent)

title: Green deterministic interrupt injection scenarios
description:
Create and green test/lockstep/test_interrupt_injection.py.

Scenarios (§16.5):
1. Deterministic interrupt injection around EI: inject IF bits at precise commit indices relative to EI execution
2. Deterministic interrupt injection around HALT: inject at various points before/during/after HALT
3. Interrupt injection around RETI: verify no double-service
4. Multiple simultaneous interrupts: verify priority ordering
5. Interrupt during multi-M-cycle instruction: verify instruction completes first
6. halt-after-ei corner cases (§16.5)
7. Pending IF injection via SimStimulus if_set_bits/if_clear_bits

All stimuli use the deterministic perturbation engine: seeded event scripts keyed by commit index. Same stream applied to both DUT and oracle (§16.5.1).

title: Register write-enable discipline verification tests
description:
Implement explicit tests verifying the register write-enable discipline from §9.4: registers update ONLY on proper write-enable conditions, never spuriously.

RATIONALE (§9.4):
'The register file should not update because the phase changed. It should update only because: an instruction writeback occurred, PC/SP progressed, an interrupt state transition occurred, or reset happened. Every other cycle should hold its value.'

This discipline is both a CORRECTNESS property (spurious writes corrupt state) and a POWER property (unnecessary toggles waste energy). Testing it explicitly catches a class of bugs where the cpu_core entity accidentally updates registers it shouldn't.

TESTS TO IMPLEMENT (test/unit/test_write_enable.py):

1. NOP hold test:
   - Execute NOP instruction
   - Verify: ONLY PC changes (PC += 1). A, F, B, C, D, E, H, L, SP all hold exactly.
   - Verify: no register write-enable is asserted for non-PC registers

2. Targeted write test (per instruction family):
   - For each instruction, verify ONLY the documented target register changes
   - Example: LD B,0x42 should change B and PC only. A, F, C, D, E, H, L, SP unchanged.
   - Example: ADD A,B should change A, F, and PC only. B, C, D, E, H, L, SP unchanged.
   - Use opcode metadata (bd-36u) to generate test parameters

3. m_ce=0 hold test (simulation complement to formal bd-1yk):
   - Set m_ce=0 for N clock cycles
   - Verify: ALL registers hold their values exactly (including PC)
   - Verify: no write-enable signals are asserted
   - This is the behavioral complement to the formal property

4. Multi-cycle instruction hold test:
   - For multi-M-cycle instructions, verify that intermediate M-cycles only change the transient microstate, not architectural registers
   - Example: LD A,(HL) takes 2 M-cycles. During M-cycle 1 (fetch), only PC changes. During M-cycle 2 (read), A changes.

5. Duty cycle measurement:
   - Over a representative instruction sequence, measure what fraction of clock cycles each register's write-enable is asserted
   - Report as a table for power analysis baseline
   - Feed results to bd-2og (register write-enable duty cycle measurement)

LOGGING FORMAT (per test case):
  [CASE] test_nop_hold
    [STEP] State before: A=0x42 F=0xB0 B=0x00 ... PC=0x0100 SP=0xFFFE
    [STEP] Execute NOP (1 M-cycle)
    [CHECK] A held: 0x42 == 0x42 OK
    [CHECK] F held: 0xB0 == 0xB0 OK
    [CHECK] B held: 0x00 == 0x00 OK
    ...
    [CHECK] PC advanced: 0x0101 == 0x0101 OK
    [CHECK] SP held: 0xFFFE == 0xFFFE OK
    [PASS] test_nop_hold

ACCEPTANCE: All tests pass. Every single-byte instruction has at least one targeted write test. Duty cycle report generated.

title: ALU types: AluReq, AluResult
description:
Create src/cpu/alu.spade with ALU type definitions from §11.1 (stub implementation).

AluResult:
  struct AluResult { val8: uint<8>, val16: uint<16>, flags: Flags }

AluReq (§11.1) — exhaustive enum of all ALU operations:
  enum AluReq {
    Idle,                              // §11.2: operand isolation — inputs stay quiet
    Add8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>, carry_in: bool},
    Sub8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>, carry_in: bool},
    And8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Or8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Xor8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Cp8{a: uint<8>, b: uint<8>},
    Inc8{x: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Dec8{x: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Add16{a: uint<16>, b: uint<16>, z_preserve: bool},
    AddSpE8{sp: uint<16>, off: uint<8>},  // signed offset, tricky flags
    Daa{a: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Cpl{a: uint<8>, f_prev: Flags},
    Scf{f_prev: Flags},
    Ccf{f_prev: Flags},
    RotShift{...},                     // RLCA, RRCA, RLA, RRA, RLC, RRC, RL, RR, SLA, SRA, SRL, SWAP
    BitResSet{...},                    // BIT, RES, SET
  }

Stub implementation:
  fn alu(req: AluReq) -> AluResult  // Returns zeros for now

The Idle case is NOT aesthetics (§11.2). It ensures that when the CPU is not performing ALU work, the ALU input buses stay quiet and large adders do not see unrelated toggling. This is a power architecture decision.

title: Green ALU_FLAGS custom ROM
description:
Write the ALU_FLAGS custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP with various operand values
- Focus on flag edge cases: zero results, half-carries, carries, borrows
- INC/DEC with carry preservation verification
- DAA after ADD and SUB sequences
- CPL, SCF, CCF flag behavior

Each test:
1. Set up registers to known state
2. Execute ALU instruction
3. Check result register AND flag register
4. Write pass/fail to WRAM signature block
5. Jump to __fail if any check fails

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Create local smoke scripts for CI-free verification
description:
Create stable local entry-point scripts in tools/ that wrap the canonical runner and tool-specific checks. These scripts are a UX layer, not a second place to encode tier selection or oracle logic.

Scripts to create:
- tools/smoke.sh  — thin wrapper around tools/run_tests.py --tier smoke plus clean-checkout tool/version checks
- tools/regress.sh — thin wrapper around tools/run_tests.py --tier full|nightly
- tools/power.sh  — wrapper for the power/activity-capture path
- tools/formal.sh — wrapper for formal verification checks
- tools/oracle.sh — direct PyBoy oracle smoke path: init, reset, symbol lookup, step, snapshot/restore

Each script should:
- Check for required tool versions
- Exit with clear error messages if tools are missing
- Support a --dry-run flag for CI integration testing
- Delegate real tier selection to tools/run_tests.py and test/tiers.yaml instead of duplicating that logic

WHY THIS SHAPE: these scripts should remain thin, stable entry points for developers working from a clean checkout. That means they intentionally wait for the canonical runner, tier definitions, and oracle interface instead of inventing a parallel control plane.

title: Green BG_SCROLL_SIGNED custom ROM
description:
Write and green BG_SCROLL_SIGNED.

Scope:
- Exercise SCX/SCY scrolling with signed-offset edge cases
- Verify wrap-around behavior and semantic pixel output
- Keep this bead separate from window and sprite concerns

Why split: scroll bugs should not block static-background completion or vice versa.

title: Bus fabric unit tests with detailed address decode verification
description:
Create test/unit/test_bus_fabric.py -- comprehensive unit tests for the minimal bus fabric (bd-2sw) with detailed address decode verification.

RATIONALE: The bus fabric is the CPU's window to memory. Address decode bugs silently corrupt state: a write intended for WRAM lands in IO, a ROM read comes from HRAM. These bugs are invisible to register-level comparison and only show up as mysterious ROM failures. Testing every address boundary prevents this.

TESTS TO IMPLEMENT:

1. ADDRESS DECODE BOUNDARY TESTS:
   For each boundary in the memory map, test addresses on both sides:
   - 0x7FFF (ROM) vs 0x8000 (Vram/unmapped in Wave A)
   - 0x9FFF (Vram) vs 0xA000 (CartRam/unmapped)
   - 0xBFFF (CartRam) vs 0xC000 (WRAM)
   - 0xDFFF (WRAM) vs 0xE000 (Echo)
   - 0xFDFF (Echo) vs 0xFE00 (OAM)
   - 0xFE9F (OAM) vs 0xFEA0 (NotUsable)
   - 0xFEFF (NotUsable) vs 0xFF00 (IO)
   - 0xFF7F (IO) vs 0xFF80 (HRAM)
   - 0xFFFE (HRAM) vs 0xFFFF (IE)

2. ROM READ TESTS:
   - Load known ROM data, read back every byte, verify match
   - Read address 0x0000 (first byte) and 0x7FFF (last ROM byte)
   - Verify ROM is read-only (writes ignored)

3. WRAM READ/WRITE TESTS:
   - Write pattern to 0xC000-0xDFFF, read back, verify
   - Test first byte (0xC000) and last byte (0xDFFF)
   - Test write-then-read in same M-cycle
   - Test address wrapping behavior

4. HRAM READ/WRITE TESTS:
   - Write pattern to 0xFF80-0xFFFE, read back, verify
   - Test boundary bytes

5. IO STUB TESTS:
   - Read from any IO address returns 0xFF
   - Writes to IO addresses are accepted without error

6. BusObs VERIFICATION:
   - For each memory region, verify BusObs.region matches expected BusRegion
   - Verify BusObs.blocked is false for all Wave A accesses
   - Verify BusObs.owner is Cpu for all CPU-initiated accesses

7. IDLE BUS BEHAVIOR:
   - BusReq::Idle produces no memory access
   - BusResp returns 0xFF
   - Memory arrays not read (power: chip-select stays low)

LOGGING FORMAT:
  [SUITE] test_bus_fabric.py
    [CASE] test_address_decode_boundary_rom_vram
      [CHECK] addr=0x7FFF region=Rom OK
      [CHECK] addr=0x8000 region=Vram OK (unmapped in Wave A, returns 0xFF)
    [CASE] test_wram_write_read
      [STEP] Write 0xAA to 0xC000
      [STEP] Read from 0xC000
      [CHECK] data=0xAA OK
    ...

ACCEPTANCE: All boundary addresses tested. All memory regions return correct BusRegion in BusObs. Read/write roundtrip works for WRAM and HRAM. ROM is read-only.

title: Epic: Bus Realism — Wave C (Phase 4.5)
description:
Implement realistic bus features: OAM DMA, memory banking (MBC1/MBC3), and bus ownership arbitration.

Components:
- OAM DMA controller: copies 160 bytes from source to OAM, CPU bus access restricted during transfer
- Bus ownership arbitration (§12.2.2): BusOwner enum {Cpu, OamDma, Ppu, Idle}
- DMA-era CPU accessibility restrictions per MemoryBehaviorProfile
- MBC1 mapper: ROM bank switching (5-bit bank), RAM bank switching (2-bit), mode select
- MBC3 mapper: ROM bank switching (7-bit bank), RAM bank switching + RTC registers

Wave C ROMs (§17.3):
- DMA_OAM_COPY: OAM DMA transfer correctness
- MBC1_SWITCH: ROM bank switching
- MBC1_RAM: external RAM enable/bank select
- MBC3_SWITCH: ROM bank switching with larger bank space
- MBC3_RAM: external RAM and RTC register access

RATIONALE: Bus realism is separated from CPU core (thesis: CPU should not directly know memory topology, §12.1). MBC mappers are the gateway to running real commercial ROMs. OAM DMA tests bus contention which is observable in real games. These are prerequisites for PPU work since PPU needs VRAM/OAM access arbitration.

ACCEPTANCE: All Wave C ROMs green. DMA transfer verified correct. Bank switching works for MBC1 and MBC3.

title: test_decode_completeness.py scaffold
description:
Create test/unit/test_decode_completeness.py (§16.2, Phase 1 step 6).

This test verifies that the decoder maps EVERY opcode to a valid DecodedOp:
- All 256 unprefixed opcodes (0x00-0xFF)
- All 256 CB-prefixed opcodes (0xCB00-0xCBFF)
- No opcode maps to an 'unknown' or 'invalid' variant unless it genuinely is (like 0xD3, 0xDB, 0xDD, 0xE3, 0xE4, 0xEB, 0xEC, 0xED, 0xF4, 0xFC, 0xFD on SM83)

Initially this test should be a scaffold that:
1. Imports the opcode metadata from spec/sm83_opcodes.py
2. For each opcode, calls the decoder and checks the result
3. FAILS until the decoder is implemented (red semantic test, not infrastructure failure)

The test should be driven from the canonical opcode metadata (§8.0), not handwritten opcode lists.

title: Test epic
description: test

title: Interrupt service phase machine (ServiceInterrupt)
description:
Implement the interrupt service sequence as a first-class phase (§8.1).

Phase:
  Phase::ServiceInterrupt { irq: IrqSel, subphase: IrqPhase }

Interrupt service sequence (5 M-cycles total):
1. Internal delay (2 M-cycles of bus idle)
2. Push PC high byte to stack (SP-1)
3. Push PC low byte to stack (SP-2)
4. Set PC to interrupt vector address

Interrupt vectors:
- VBlank:   0x0040
- LCD STAT: 0x0048
- Timer:    0x0050
- Serial:   0x0058
- Joypad:   0x0060

During service:
- IME set to Disabled
- The corresponding IF bit is cleared (via IrqAck to peripheral layer)
- SP decremented by 2

Priority: lower bit number = higher priority. If multiple interrupts pending, service the lowest-numbered one first.

The IrqPhase subphase enum tracks progress through the multi-M-cycle service sequence.

title: Green BG_STATIC, BG_SCROLL_SIGNED, PPU_WINDOW, PPU_SPRITES ROMs
description:
Write and green the Wave D1 visual test ROMs (§17.4).

BG_STATIC:
- Load a known tile pattern and tilemap
- Verify rendered pixels match expected output
- Use semantic tilemap/tile data checks, NOT frame hash alone

BG_SCROLL_SIGNED:
- Test background scrolling with SCX/SCY
- Include signed offset arithmetic edge cases
- Verify wrap-around behavior

PPU_WINDOW:
- Test window rendering at various WX/WY positions
- Verify window overlays background correctly
- Test window enable/disable via LCDC bit 5

PPU_SPRITES:
- Test sprite rendering with various attributes
- Verify priority ordering (X position, OAM index)
- Test flip flags
- Test 8x16 sprite mode
- Test BG priority flag
- Test sprite transparency (color 0)

Oracle mode: frame_semantic first, then frame_hash as coarsest checksum (§17.4)
close_reason: Superseded by split Wave D1 ROM beads: BG_STATIC, BG_SCROLL_SIGNED, PPU_WINDOW, PPU_SPRITES

title: Epic: Timer & Interrupt System — Wave B (Phase 4)
description:
Implement timer, interrupt controller, and all interrupt-related CPU behaviors. Bring Wave B ROMs to green.

Timer (§12.4 Wave B):
- DIV register (free-running 16-bit counter, upper 8 bits readable, write resets to 0)
- TIMA/TMA/TAC: configurable timer with overflow interrupt
- Timer derives from shared timebase (§6.1.1), NOT private local counter
- Timer falling-edge detection for obscure increment timing

Interrupts:
- IE/IF registers live in peripheral/io side, NOT inside CPU (§12.3)
- CPU receives narrow pending vector, emits acknowledge
- IME state machine: Disabled → PendingEnable → Enabled (§8.3)
- EI delay: exactly one instruction later (§16.5 invariant)
- RETI: return + enable interrupts atomically
- ServiceInterrupt phase with proper architectural sequence (§8.1)
- HALT: Running/Halted/HaltBugPending (§8.3) — HaltBugPending can be deferred but type must accommodate it

Wave B ROMs (§17.2):
- TIMER_DIV_BASIC: DIV counting, reset-on-write, TAC prescaler selection
- EI_DELAY: EI enables interrupts one instruction late
- TIMER_IRQ_HALT: timer overflow fires IRQ, wakes from HALT

Additional verification:
- Deterministic interrupt injection scenarios (§16.5)
- halt-after-ei corner cases
- HALT wake behavior matches pending IE & IF
- Interrupt service consumes correct architectural sequence
- JOY_DIVERGE_PERSIST: joypad input divergence with persistent state (requires SimStimulus joyp_buttons)

RATIONALE: Interrupts and timers are the second-highest source of subtle CPU bugs after ALU flags. The plan (§8.3) insists they be first-class phase states, not conditionals buried in fetch. Making IE/IF external to the CPU (§12.3) is a modularity decision that simplifies later PPU/timer integration. The shared timebase (§6.1.1) prevents timer/CPU/DMA blocks from drifting by one cycle.
