ACCEPTANCE (Milestone D): EI_DELAY and TIMER_IRQ_HALT green. Live instruction-commit differential green on Wave B. Strict M-cycle differential green on targeted subsets. Every interrupt source observed in at least one green test. HALT wake confirmed.

title: Register write-enable duty cycle measurement
description:
Implement always-on logical quiescence metrics (§18.7 tier 1).

Metrics to track:
1. Register write-enable duty cycle: for each register (A,F,B,C,D,E,H,L,SP,PC), count cycles where write-enable is asserted vs total cycles
2. Bus-active duty cycle: fraction of cycles where BusReq != Idle
3. ALU-active duty cycle: fraction of cycles where AluReq != Idle
4. Halt quiescence ratio: fraction of halted cycles with truly zero activity

Implementation:
- Add counters to simulation tops (NOT hardware tops!)
- Report duty cycles after each test run
- Flag anomalies: e.g., PC write-enable > 50% is suspicious, register write-enable during HALT is a bug

These metrics are the 'canary in the coal mine' for power regressions. Track them in CI.

title: Bus types: BusReq, BusResp, BusObs, BusOwner, BusRegion

title: Minimal bus fabric: ROM + WRAM + HRAM + dummy IO
description:
Implement the minimal bus fabric for Wave A CPU bring-up (plan section 12.4 Wave A).

This fabric provides just enough memory infrastructure for the first ROMs to execute. It does NOT include DMA, MBC, PPU, or contention logic--those come in later waves.

MEMORY MAP IMPLEMENTATION (src/bus/membus.spade):
- 0x0000-0x7FFF: ROM (read-only, loaded from test ROM)
- 0xC000-0xDFFF: WRAM (8KB, read/write)
- 0xFF80-0xFFFE: HRAM (127 bytes, read/write)
- 0xFF00-0xFF7F: IO registers (dummy reads return 0xFF, writes ignored for now)
- All other addresses: return 0xFF on read, ignore writes

INTERFACE:
  entity membus(
      clk: clock,
      rst: bool,
      m_ce: bool,
      req: BusReq,
  ) -> (BusResp, BusObs)

  - BusReq::Idle -> BusResp { data: 0xFF }, BusObs { region: Hram, blocked: false, owner: Idle }
  - BusReq::Read { addr } -> BusResp { data: mem[addr] }, BusObs { region: decode(addr), ... }
  - BusReq::Write { addr, data } -> write mem[addr] if writable, BusObs { ... }

MEMORY IMPLEMENTATION:
- ROM: simulation loads ROM data into an inferred memory array (synthesis will use SPRAM later, bd-23s)
- WRAM: inferred memory array (8KB). For simulation this is fine; hardware mapping to SPRAM comes in bd-23s
- HRAM: register array (127 bytes, small enough for registers)
- IO stubs: return 0xFF for reads, discard writes. Individual IO registers are wired in as peripherals are added (timer in bd-3pw, IE/IF in bd-gsm, etc.)

ADDRESS DECODE:
The address decode logic maps the 16-bit address to BusRegion for the BusObs sideband (bd-3pj). This is tested by bd-2an (bus fabric unit tests) which verifies every address boundary.

POWER CONSIDERATIONS:
- When BusReq::Idle, memory arrays should not be read (chip-select low)
- Write strobes false on Idle and Read
- Address decode logic should be clean enough to not create unnecessary fanout

WHY MINIMAL: The plan (section 12.4 Wave A) explicitly limits the first bus to ROM/WRAM/HRAM/dummy-IO. This prevents peripheral complexity from obscuring CPU bugs (Risk 5 mitigation). Wave B adds timer/interrupt IO, Wave C adds MBC/DMA, Wave D adds VRAM/OAM.

ACCEPTANCE: All address boundaries decode correctly. ROM loads and reads back. WRAM read/write works. HRAM read/write works. IO reads return 0xFF. BusObs emitted correctly.

title: Unified test runner with hierarchical logging and coverage tracking
description:
Create tools/run_tests.py -- the single-command entry point for running all verification tiers with comprehensive logging and coverage tracking.

TEST TIERS (executed in order, each tier independently runnable):
1. META/INFRASTRUCTURE: Spade-Cocotb integration, oracle init, event determinism, reset profile
2. UNIT TESTS: ALU, decoder, register file, flag helpers, timebase, bus fabric, write-enable discipline

5. LOCKSTEP: Live instruction-commit and M-cycle-commit comparison
6. INVARIANT: Metamorphic tests, CPU invariant tests
7. POWER: Halt quiescence, ALU idle isolation, write-enable duty cycle

COMMAND INTERFACE:
  python tools/run_tests.py                        # run all tiers
  python tools/run_tests.py --tier unit            # run one tier
  python tools/run_tests.py --tier unit,rom        # run multiple tiers

    ...

  Phase constructors: 8/10 (80%) -- GAPS: ServiceInterrupt, Halted

  Interrupt causes: 0/5 (0%) -- Phase 4 not yet implemented

FEATURES:
- Parallel test execution within tiers (configurable parallelism)
- Automatic failure artifact collection
- JUnit XML output for CI integration

title: Divergence artifact emission framework
description:
Implement the divergence artifact framework (§16.7) for when live oracle comparison fails.

When a CommitTrace comparison fails, emit:
1. The first bad CommitTrace (DUT vs oracle)
2. A commit window before/after the failure (configurable, default ±5)
3. A minimized replay capsule (see replay capsule task)
4. A localized waveform dump (VCD/FST) centered on the failure
5. Optional in-memory oracle state snapshot

Artifact output:
- Human-readable summary to stderr
- Machine-readable JSON to artifact directory
- Waveform file for waveform viewer (surfer, GTKWave)

This framework is critical for debugging. When a test fails, the engineer should be able to:
1. Read the summary to understand WHAT diverged
2. Load the replay capsule to reproduce it
3. Open the waveform to see the signal-level picture
4. Inspect the oracle snapshot to understand the expected state

title: cpu_core semantic architecture: step_mcycle + apply_delta framework
description:
Implement the two pure functions that form the semantic heart of the CPU (§7.2-7.3), and wire them into the cpu_core entity.

This is THE most architecturally significant task in the entire project. The plan (§24) says: 'Build the CPU as a pure, typed M-cycle transition function wrapped by a thin clocked Spade entity.'

FUNCTIONS TO IMPLEMENT:

1. fn step_mcycle(state: CpuState, input: MicroInput) -> MicroOutput
   - Takes current CpuState (arch + micro) and external inputs (bus_resp, irq_pending)
   - Returns MicroOutput containing: CpuDelta, BusReq, CommitTrace, sideband
   - Implemented as a large match on state.micro.phase:
     Phase::Fetch => handle_fetch(state, input)
     Phase::FetchPrefix { prefix } => handle_fetch_prefix(state, input, prefix)
     Phase::ReadImm8 { k } => handle_read_imm8(state, input, k)
     Phase::ReadImm16Lo { k } => ...
     Phase::ReadImm16Hi { lo, k } => ...
     Phase::ReadMem { addr, k } => handle_read_mem(state, input, addr, k)
     Phase::WriteMem { addr, data, k } => handle_write_mem(state, input, addr, data, k)
     Phase::Execute { op } => handle_execute(state, input, op)
     Phase::ServiceInterrupt { irq, subphase } => handle_interrupt(state, input, irq, subphase)
     Phase::Halted => handle_halted(state, input)
   - Each arm delegates to the phase machine handlers from bd-4tk, bd-3ab, bd-3o1, bd-1je, bd-25t

2. fn apply_delta(state: CpuState, delta: CpuDelta) -> CpuState
   - Pure function that applies sparse register writes from CpuDelta to CpuState
   - CpuDelta contains Option fields: only Some values cause writes
   - This function is used by: (a) the cpu_core entity to commit state on m_ce, and (b) test reference models for offline comparison
   - The sparse delta pattern is critical for power (§18.4): it ensures only targeted registers toggle

3. cpu_core entity integration:
   - Owns registers storing CpuState via reg(clk) with explicit hold behavior
   - On each clock edge where m_ce is true:
     a. Evaluate step_mcycle(current_state, input) combinationally
     b. Apply delta to state via apply_delta
     c. Emit BusReq and CommitTrace outputs
   - On m_ce=false: ALL architectural state holds. No toggles. This is the power contract.

WHY THIS ARCHITECTURE:
- step_mcycle is the DENOTATION of one machine step (§7.3). It answers: given this state and input, what happens?
- apply_delta is the OPERATIONAL commit mechanism. It answers: how does state update?
- Separating them means tests can call step_mcycle without a clock, and formal tools can reason about the pure function independently from the entity wrapper.
- The entity wrapper is deliberately thin: it owns registers and the m_ce gate, nothing else.

FILE LOCATIONS:
- src/cpu/semantics.spade: step_mcycle, apply_delta, and the top-level phase dispatch
- src/cpu/core.spade: cpu_core entity (updates the existing stub from bd-1o2)

RELATIONSHIP TO PHASE MACHINE TASKS:
This task creates the FRAMEWORK (the match dispatch and the delta/entity infrastructure). The individual phase machine tasks (bd-4tk, bd-3ab, bd-3o1, bd-1je, bd-25t, bd-13p) fill in the match arms. This task should be done FIRST so that phase machine tasks have a structure to plug into.

ACCEPTANCE: cpu_core compiles, step_mcycle dispatches to at least one phase handler, apply_delta correctly applies sparse deltas, m_ce=false produces no state change.

title: Author Wave A custom ROM assembly source
description:
Write the SM83 assembly source files for all 7 Wave A custom test ROMs (§17.1). Each ROM follows the checkpoint ABI and targets specific CPU instruction families WITHOUT requiring timers, interrupts, DMA, MBC, or PPU.

ROM SPECIFICATIONS:

1. LOADS_BASIC (bench/roms/loads_basic.asm):
   Tests: LD r,r / LD r,n8 / LD r,(HL) / LD (HL),r / LD (HL),n8 / LD A,(BC) / LD A,(DE) / LD (BC),A / LD (DE),A / LD A,(nn) / LD (nn),A / LD r16,nn / LD SP,HL / LD (nn),SP / PUSH/POP pairs for load verification
   Strategy: Load known values into registers, store to WRAM, read back and verify. Each test case writes pass/fail to WRAM signature block.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_ld_r_r, __checkpoint_ld_r_n8, __checkpoint_ld_mem, __checkpoint_ld_r16

2. ALU_FLAGS (bench/roms/alu_flags.asm):
   Tests: ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/OR/XOR/CP with edge cases for every flag (Z, N, H, C)
   Strategy: For each ALU op, test: zero result, non-zero result, half-carry, full carry, negative flag. Verify flags by pushing AF to stack and checking F byte.
   Critical edge cases: ADC with carry_in=1, SBC with carry_in=1, half-carry on 0x0F+0x01, carry on 0xFF+0x01
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_add, __checkpoint_adc, __checkpoint_sub, __checkpoint_sbc, __checkpoint_logic

3. ALU16_SP (bench/roms/alu16_sp.asm):
   Tests: ADD HL,rr / ADD SP,e8 / LD HL,SP+e8 / INC rr / DEC rr
   Strategy: These have the trickiest flag behavior in the SM83. Flags for ADD SP,e8 and LD HL,SP+e8 are based on low byte arithmetic (not the 16-bit result), which is counterintuitive.
   Critical cases from gbxcule (§11.3): SP=0xFFF8 + e8=0x08 (H=1,C=1), SP=0x0000 + e8=0xFF (signed -1)
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_add_hl, __checkpoint_add_sp_e8, __checkpoint_ld_hl_sp_e8

4. FLOW_STACK (bench/roms/flow_stack.asm):
   Tests: JP nn / JP cc,nn / JR e8 / JR cc,e8 / CALL nn / CALL cc,nn / RET / RET cc / RST vec / PUSH rr / POP rr
   Strategy: Test each control flow instruction with both taken and not-taken conditions. Verify SP changes correctly for PUSH/POP/CALL/RET. Test RST vectors 0x00-0x38.
   Critical cases: conditional branches with all flag conditions (Z/NZ/C/NC), nested CALL/RET, SP underflow
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_jp, __checkpoint_jr, __checkpoint_call_ret, __checkpoint_rst

5. CB_BITOPS (bench/roms/cb_bitops.asm):
   Tests: RLC/RRC/RL/RR/SLA/SRA/SWAP/SRL for all registers + (HL) / BIT b,r / RES b,r / SET b,r
   Strategy: For each rotate/shift, test with known bit patterns (0x80, 0x01, 0x00, 0xFF) and verify result + flags. BIT tests all 8 bit positions. RES/SET verify single-bit modification.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_rotate, __checkpoint_shift, __checkpoint_bit, __checkpoint_res_set

6. MEM_RWB (bench/roms/mem_rwb.asm):
   Tests: Soak test writing patterns to WRAM and reading back
   Strategy: Write ascending/descending/checkerboard patterns to WRAM region, read back all and compare. Tests bus fabric address decode, WRAM read/write, and memory stability.
   Covers: bus correctness over sustained operation

7. ALU_LOOP (bench/roms/alu_loop.asm):
   Tests: Stress test looping ALU operations
   Strategy: Iterative loops performing ALU ops with accumulating state. Verify final accumulator/flag state matches expected. Tests CPU under sustained instruction throughput.
   Covers: interaction between control flow and ALU over many iterations

COMMON STRUCTURE (all ROMs):
  SECTION "Header", ROM0[$100]
  jp Start
  ds $150 - @, 0  ; Nintendo logo area (rgbfix fills this)
  
  SECTION "Main", ROM0[$150]
  Start:
    di                    ; Disable interrupts
    ld sp, $FFFE          ; Initialize stack
    ; ... test code ...
    ; Write WRAM signature block
    jp __pass / jp __fail
  
  SECTION "Pass", ROM0[$0300]
  __pass: jr __pass       ; Infinite loop on success
  
  SECTION "Fail", ROM0[$0310]  
  __fail: jr __fail       ; Infinite loop on failure

ACCEPTANCE: All 7 ROMs assemble with rgbds. All pass validate_rom_abi.py. All .sym files contain __pass and __fail labels. All ROMs are registered in bench/manifests/roms.yaml with correct oracle_mode and compare_scope.

title: Debug-free hardware build configuration
description:
Implement compile-time or top-level mechanism to exclude debug/simulation signals from hardware builds (§18.6).

PROBLEM: Simulation tops expose CommitTrace, DebugTrace, BusObs, and SimStimulus ports. If these accidentally leak into the hardware synthesis, they inflate LUT/DFF counts and toggle rates, contaminating power measurements.

SOLUTION OPTIONS:
1. Separate top-level modules (PREFERRED): simulation tops in src/sim/, hardware top in src/board/. Synthesis targets only src/board/icebreaker_top.spade.
2. Spade conditional compilation: if Spade supports compile-time flags, use them to strip debug ports.
3. Yosys post-synthesis verification: script that checks the synthesized netlist for debug signal names.

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Verify that icebreaker_top.spade does not instantiate or reference any type from src/sim/
- Create tools/verify_hw_build.sh that:
  1. Synthesizes icebreaker_top with yosys
  2. Greps the synthesized JSON netlist for debug signal patterns (CommitTrace, DebugTrace, BusObs, SimStimulus)
  3. Fails if any debug signals found
  4. Reports resource utilization

TESTING:
- Run verify_hw_build.sh as part of the synthesis smoke check
- Verify LUT count is NOT inflated by debug fanout
- Compare resource utilization between debug-free and debug-included builds

ACCEPTANCE: Hardware build contains ZERO simulation-only signals. Resource counts match expectations for a clean design.

title: SAIF activity capture on representative ROM windows

Implement nightly activity captures (§18.7 tier 2).

- Compare against baseline

SAIF captures provide the ground truth for dynamic power estimation. They show exactly which signals toggle how often during real workloads.

- Comparison against baseline (regression detection)

These should run in the nightly regression, not in the fast CI loop.

title: Define model, reset, and memory behavior profile enums
description:
Define the three orthogonal profile enums that parameterize ALL simulation and testing (§13).

ModelProfile (§13.1):
- DMG: original Game Boy
- CGB: Game Boy Color (future, but type must exist now)

ResetProfile (§13.1):
- SkipBoot: post-boot register/memory state, no boot ROM execution
- RawPowerOn: true power-on state (requires boot ROM)

MemoryBehaviorProfile (§13.1.1):
- DmgConservative: safe defaults for all ambiguous behaviors
- DmgRevisionSpecific: matches a specific DMG hardware revision

The MemoryBehaviorProfile fixes:
- Echo RAM mirroring policy (0xE000-0xFDFF mirrors 0xC000-0xDDFF)
- 0xFEA0-0xFEFF read behavior (prohibited region)
- Blocked VRAM/OAM read/write policy during PPU access
- DMA-era CPU accessibility restrictions

For CPU bring-up: pin ModelProfile::DMG and ResetProfile::SkipBoot. Record these in replay capsules and ROM manifests.

These profiles should be defined in BOTH:
1. Python (spec/compare_scopes.py or similar) for oracle/harness use
2. Spade (src/cpu/types.spade or similar) for HDL parameterization

RATIONALE: §13 calls this 'one of the highest-risk correctness traps.' If reset state is wrong, every test result is suspect. Making profiles explicit and recording them in replay capsules makes divergences diagnosable.

title: E2E milestone gate test: Milestone A -- harness ready
description:
Create tools/gate_milestone_a.sh -- the acceptance gate script for Milestone A (§22: Harness Ready).

This script validates ALL Milestone A acceptance criteria in one invocation. It must be runnable from a clean checkout and produce a clear PASS/FAIL verdict with detailed logging.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (§22 Milestone A):
1. Generators run reproducibly: run test_alu_generated_vectors.py twice with same seed, diff outputs
2. Local smoke scripts run from clean checkout: tools/smoke.sh oracle, cocotb, synth, formal all exit 0
3. Harness tests green: test_decode_completeness.py, test_reset_profile.py, test_event_script_determinism.py pass
4. HDL tests execute and fail SEMANTICALLY, not infrastructurally: test_cpu_lockstep.py runs, fails with value mismatch (not import error, compile error, or timeout)

SCRIPT STRUCTURE:
  #!/bin/bash
  set -euo pipefail
  PASS=0; FAIL=0
  
  echo "=== MILESTONE A GATE: Harness Ready ==="
  
  echo "--- Criterion 1: Generator reproducibility ---"
  # Run generators twice with seed=42, compare output
  
  echo "--- Criterion 2: Smoke scripts ---"
  # Run each smoke script, collect exit codes
  
  echo "--- Criterion 3: Harness tests ---"
  # Run harness test suite, check all green
  
  echo "--- Criterion 4: Semantic failure ---"
  # Run lockstep test, verify it fails with xfail (not crash)
  
  echo "=== MILESTONE A: $PASS passed, $FAIL failed ==="

LOGGING: Each criterion produces [GATE PASS] or [GATE FAIL] with explanation. On failure, the specific failing component and error message are shown.

WHY GATE TESTS: The plan defines milestones (§22) as sequential quality gates. Without automated gate tests, milestone claims are subjective. Gate tests make milestones objectively verifiable and provide a clear signal for when to proceed to the next phase.

ACCEPTANCE: Script exits 0 when all criteria pass, non-zero on any failure. Each criterion independently testable.

title: Timebase entity: T-cycle and M-cycle enables
description:
Create src/board/clockgen.spade with the canonical architectural timebase entity from §6.1.1.

Three clocks (§6.1):
1. board clock: physical iCEBreaker input clock
2. T-cycle: Game Boy timing quantum
3. M-cycle: 4 T-cycles

Timebase entity outputs:
  entity timebase(clk: clock, rst: bool) -> TimebaseOut {
    t_ce: bool,       // T-cycle clock enable
    m_ce: bool,       // M-cycle clock enable (every 4th T-cycle)
    sys_counter: uint<N>,  // canonical system counter
    t_index: uint<2>,      // 0-3 within current M-cycle
    m_index: uint<N>,      // M-cycle count
  }

DESIGN PRINCIPLES:
- CPU commits on m_ce, but cycle-sensitive devices derive edge timing from this shared source (§6.1.1)
- Timer and DIV derive from this shared counter, NOT private local counters
- This prevents timer, CPU, and DMA blocks from drifting by one cycle
- First milestone: m_ce can be asserted every N simulation clocks or even every clock (§6.5)
- Do NOT hardcode exact real-time frequency in first CPU milestone — logical correctness first
- Do NOT use fabric-gated clocks (§6.4) — single root clock + enables
- Harness may accelerate/decelerate m_ce but may NOT skip/synthesize architectural counter states

title: Deterministic perturbation engine
description:
Implement the seeded deterministic perturbation engine (§16.5.1) in bench/actions/generators.py.

Core principle: ALL external stimuli are emitted from a seeded event script keyed by commit index or checkpoint symbol. The SAME event stream is applied to BOTH the DUT and the oracle. Events are NEVER keyed by wall-clock time.

Event types (matching SimStimulus from §12.6):
- Joypad button state changes (joyp_buttons)
- Interrupt flag set/clear (if_set_bits, if_clear_bits)
- IE override (ie_override)
- DMA start trigger (dma_start)
- Serial data injection (serial_inject)
- Architectural time freeze (freeze_arch_time)
- CPU-only hold (cpu_hold_only)

Generator interface:
- SeededEventScript: deterministic sequence from seed + ROM manifest
- Supports both hand-authored event lists and generated sequences
- Every event is (commit_index: int, event: SimEvent)

Do NOT use ad hoc hierarchical forcing (force/release) for ordinary test stimuli (§12.6). Reserve force/release for exceptional fault injection only.

title: Machine-readable opcode metadata source (sm83_opcodes.py)
description:
Create spec/sm83_opcodes.py — the single canonical opcode metadata source (§8.0).

This file is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for static instruction facts. It must contain:
- opcode byte (0x00-0xFF for unprefixed, 0x00-0xFF for CB-prefix)
- prefix class (none, CB)
- mnemonic (e.g., 'LD B,C', 'ADD A,(HL)', 'BIT 3,H')
- instruction length in bytes (1, 2, or 3)
- cycle tuple(s): (not-taken, taken) for conditional ops, single value otherwise
- flag policy: which flags are affected and how (Z, N, H, C: set/reset/affected/unchanged)
- operand and addressing forms
- default compare scope for differential testing

Format: Python data structure (list of dicts or dataclass instances) that can be:
1. Imported by test generators (test_decode_completeness.py, test_alu_generated_vectors.py)
2. Used to generate Spade lookup tables if needed
3. Used to drive coverage tracking across opcode families

Sources to cross-reference: Pan Docs, gbxcule templates, Gekkio's opcode tables.

The phase machine remains hand-authored (§8.0). Only static instruction facts come from this file.

title: ALU operand isolation (Idle case)
description:
Verify and enforce ALU operand isolation via AluReq::Idle (§11.2).

When AluReq::Idle:
- All ALU input operands should be zero/stable (not receiving toggling data)
- The ALU result should be a defined default (zeros)
- Large adders should not see unrelated toggling

Implementation:
- Ensure the match arm for Idle returns a known-zero AluResult
- Ensure the CPU control logic emits AluReq::Idle in all phases that don't need ALU computation
- Add a test that verifies Idle produces stable outputs

This is a POWER architecture decision (§11.2, §18.2), not aesthetics. In a real FPGA, toggling adder inputs wastes dynamic power even if the result is unused.

title: MBC1 mapper (ROM/RAM bank switching)
description:
Implement MBC1 memory bank controller.

MBC1 registers (memory-mapped, no reads):
- 0x0000-0x1FFF: RAM Enable (0x0A enables, other values disable)
- 0x2000-0x3FFF: ROM Bank Number (5 bits, bank 0 maps to 1)
- 0x4000-0x5FFF: RAM Bank Number / Upper ROM Bank (2 bits)
- 0x6000-0x7FFF: Banking Mode Select (0=ROM mode, 1=RAM mode)

Address mapping:
- 0x0000-0x3FFF: ROM bank 0 (or bank N*0x20 in RAM mode)
- 0x4000-0x7FFF: ROM bank N (5-bit + optional 2-bit upper)
- 0xA000-0xBFFF: External RAM (if enabled, 8KB per bank, up to 4 banks)

Special cases:
- ROM bank 0x00 maps to 0x01 (can never select bank 0 in upper region)
- Similarly 0x20→0x21, 0x40→0x41, 0x60→0x61
- RAM mode changes which ROM bank appears in 0x0000-0x3FFF

title: Phase machine for ALU instructions
description:
Implement phase machine for 8-bit ALU instruction families in src/cpu/control.spade and src/cpu/semantics.spade.

Instruction families:
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP A,r: single M-cycle, ALU op on register operand
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP A,n: fetch immediate, then ALU op
- ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP A,(HL): read from (HL), then ALU op
- INC/DEC r: single M-cycle with flag-preserving behavior
- INC/DEC (HL): read-modify-write at (HL) — read, ALU, write back
- DAA/CPL/SCF/CCF: single M-cycle, misc ALU ops on A
- RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA: single M-cycle, accumulator rotates (UNPREFIXED — distinct from CB-prefix rotates!)
  CRITICAL: These set Z=0 ALWAYS, unlike CB-prefix rotates which set Z based on result. This is one of the most common implementation bugs. The ALU must handle both variants correctly.

Phase transitions:
- Register-register: Fetch → Execute (1 M-cycle)
- Immediate: Fetch → ReadImm8{k: AluFromImm} → Execute (2 M-cycles)
- Memory: Fetch → ReadMem{addr: HL, k: AluFromMem{kind}} → Execute (2 M-cycles)
- INC/DEC (HL): Fetch → ReadMem → ALU → WriteMem → Done (3 M-cycles)
- Accumulator rotates: Fetch → Execute (1 M-cycle, uses ALU with Z-force-clear flag)

The ALU is invoked via AluReq — the phase machine selects the correct AluReq variant based on DecodedOp. For RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA, the phase machine must use a variant that forces Z=0 (or the ALU must have a separate accumulator-rotate variant).

TESTING: Each instruction family needs at least one curated test case. The RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA Z-flag behavior MUST have explicit test cases comparing against CB-prefix equivalents to catch the most common GB CPU implementation bug.

title: ALU implementation: DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF
description:
Implement miscellaneous ALU operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- DAA: Decimal Adjust Accumulator (BCD correction)
- CPL: Complement A (bitwise NOT), flags N=1, H=1, Z/C preserved
- SCF: Set Carry Flag, flags N=0, H=0, C=1, Z preserved
- CCF: Complement Carry Flag, flags N=0, H=0, C=!C, Z preserved

TRICKY: DAA (§11.3) is the single most complex ALU operation:
- Behavior depends on N flag (was last op add or subtract?)
- Behavior depends on H flag and C flag from previous operation
- Behavior depends on current value of A
- After ADD/ADC (N=0): if A>0x99 or C, add 0x60 and set C; if (A&0xF)>9 or H, add 0x06
- After SUB/SBC (N=1): if C, subtract 0x60; if H, subtract 0x06
- Z flag set if result==0, N preserved, H=0, C set if correction overflowed

Do NOT re-derive DAA from memory. Translate the exact gbxcule formula.

title: CPU core single-instruction differential tests
description:
Create test/unit/test_cpu_single_op.py — targeted single-instruction differential tests that verify individual instructions against the PyBoy oracle WITHOUT requiring full ROM execution.

PURPOSE: ROM tests (LOADS_BASIC etc.) test many instructions together. When a ROM fails, it can be hard to isolate WHICH instruction is broken. These tests isolate individual instructions and provide maximally detailed diagnostics.

TEST APPROACH:
For each instruction:
1. Initialize DUT and oracle to identical known state (SkipBoot reset profile)
2. Place a single instruction (+ operands) at the current PC in ROM
3. Place a HALT or infinite loop after the instruction
4. Step DUT through the instruction (known number of M-cycles from opcode metadata)
5. Step oracle through the same instruction
6. Compare full register state, flags, PC, SP, bus activity

LOGGING (per instruction test):
  [TEST] LD B,0x42 (opcode 0x06, 2 bytes, 2 M-cycles)
    [BEFORE] PC=0x0100 A=0x00 F=0x00 B=0x00 C=0x00 DE=0x0000 HL=0x0000 SP=0xFFFE
    [M-CYCLE 1] phase=Fetch bus=Read(0x0100) resp=0x06
    [M-CYCLE 2] phase=ReadImm8 bus=Read(0x0101) resp=0x42
    [AFTER]  PC=0x0102 A=0x00 F=0x00 B=0x42 C=0x00 DE=0x0000 HL=0x0000 SP=0xFFFE
    [ORACLE] PC=0x0102 A=0x00 F=0x00 B=0x42 C=0x00 DE=0x0000 HL=0x0000 SP=0xFFFE
    [PASS] All fields match

INSTRUCTION COVERAGE (parameterized):
- At least one representative from each opcode family
- Driven by the canonical opcode metadata (spec/sm83_opcodes.py)
- Flag-affecting instructions get additional parameterized inputs to test flag transitions

CORNER CASES TO EXPLICITLY TEST:
- LD (HL),n: verify the memory write occurs at the correct address
- POP AF: verify F low nibble is masked to zero
- RLCA vs CB RLC A: verify Z flag difference (the #1 GB CPU implementation bug)
- ADD SP,e8 with negative offset: verify signed extension and flag behavior
- DAA after various flag states: the trickiest flag computation in the entire instruction set
- Conditional branches: verify cycle count differs between taken/not-taken
- INC/DEC (HL): verify read-modify-write sequence

RELATIONSHIP TO ROM TESTS: These are FINER-GRAINED than ROM tests. If LOADS_BASIC fails, run these tests to pinpoint which LD variant is broken. These tests run faster (no ROM build needed) and give more detail.

title: Verify roms/build_micro_rom.py works
close_reason: Verified roms/build_micro_rom.py: fails in bare repo due to missing gbxcule, but succeeds with PYTHONPATH=/Users/bkase/Documents/gbxcule/src; generated 23 deterministic ROMs with valid checksums.

title: ALU implementation: 8-bit arithmetic (ADD, ADC, SUB, SBC, CP)
description:
Implement 8-bit arithmetic operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- ADD A,r/n: A + operand, flags Z=result==0, N=0, H=half-carry, C=carry
- ADC A,r/n: A + operand + carry_in, same flags but H/C include carry_in
- SUB r/n: A - operand, flags Z=result==0, N=1, H=half-borrow, C=borrow
- SBC A,r/n: A - operand - carry_in, same flags but H/C include carry_in
- CP r/n: same as SUB but result discarded, only flags set

TRICKY FLAG CASES (§11.3, lift from gbxcule):
- ADC half-carry: (A & 0xF) + (operand & 0xF) + carry_in > 0xF
- ADC carry: A + operand + carry_in > 0xFF (use wider arithmetic)
- SBC half-borrow: (A & 0xF) - (operand & 0xF) - carry_in < 0 (signed)
- SBC borrow: A - operand - carry_in < 0

Do NOT re-derive from memory. Translate actual gbxcule formulas.
Verify with generated vectors from spec/flag_policies.py.

title: Placeholder lockstep test as xfail
description:
Create test/lockstep/test_cpu_lockstep.py as xfail(strict=True) (Phase 1 step 11).

This test will eventually run live CPU lockstep comparison between the Spade DUT and the PyBoy oracle. Initially it should:
1. Exist as a proper test file
2. Be marked xfail(strict=True) — it MUST fail, and if it accidentally passes, that's a test error
3. Attempt to: load a simple ROM, reset both DUT and oracle, step one instruction, compare CommitTrace
4. Fail because the HDL stub doesn't produce correct values

The xfail is removed in Phase 4 when the private step adapter is ready (Phase 4 step 32).

IMPORTANT (§16.6): Keep the live oracle interface and replay capsule format stable from day one. If strict M-cycle stepping isn't wired up yet, gate only instruction-boundary suites. Do NOT introduce a file-trace fallback as a temporary workaround.

title: Promote strict lockstep from xfail to targeted green subset

Remove the xfail from test_cpu_lockstep.py for a targeted owned Wave B subset.

Scope:
- Promote strict instruction/M-cycle lockstep from placeholder to expected-pass on deliberately instrumented ROMs such as EI_DELAY and TIMER_IRQ_HALT
- Keep strict lockstep limited to owned ROMs with symbols/hooks
- Leave broader suites at coarser-grained comparison modes until they are intentionally promoted

Why add this bead: Phase 4 step 32 in the plan is a real milestone and should not be hidden inside the placeholder xfail task.

title: Python reference specs: flag_policies.py and compare_scopes.py
description:
Create the spec/ directory Python reference implementations that serve as truth sources for test generation and oracle comparison.

FILES:
- spec/flag_policies.py: Canonical flag computation formulas for every ALU operation, lifted directly from gbxcule templates (§11.3). This is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for flag behavior. Used by test_alu_generated_vectors.py to generate exhaustive test vectors. Every ALU Spade implementation must match these formulas exactly.
  - ADD/ADC: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=((a&0xF)+(b&0xF)+carry>0xF), C=(result>0xFF)
  - SUB/SBC/CP: Z=(result==0), N=1, H=((a&0xF)-(b&0xF)-carry<0), C=(a-b-carry<0)
  - AND: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=1, C=0
  - OR/XOR: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=0, C=0
  - INC: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=((val&0xF)+1>0xF), C=preserved
  - DEC: Z=(result==0), N=1, H=((val&0xF)-1<0), C=preserved
  - DAA: complex (depends on N, H, C flags — must match gbxcule exactly)
  - 16-bit ADD HL: Z=preserved, N=0, H=(bit 11 carry), C=(bit 15 carry)
  - ADD SP,e8 / LD HL,SP+e8: Z=0, N=0, H=(low byte half-carry), C=(low byte carry)
  - Rotates (CB): Z=(result==0), N=0, H=0, C=rotated-out bit
  - Rotates (unprefixed RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA): Z=0 ALWAYS, N=0, H=0, C=rotated-out bit
  - BIT: Z=(bit==0), N=0, H=1, C=preserved
  - SWAP: Z=(result==0), N=0, H=0, C=0

- spec/compare_scopes.py: Defines what to compare at each oracle comparison granularity (§7.2 CommitKind). Maps OracleMode to the set of fields checked. E.g., Unit mode checks only targeted register; InstrCommit checks full register state + flags; MCycleCommit checks everything including bus activity.

WHY SEPARATE FROM OPCODE METADATA: sm83_opcodes.py (bd-36u) has opcode structure (encoding, length, cycles). flag_policies.py has the actual flag COMPUTATION FORMULAS. compare_scopes.py has the comparison STRATEGY. Three orthogonal concerns.

title: Author Wave C custom ROM assembly source
description:
Write SM83 assembly for Wave C ROMs (§17.3) testing bus realism: OAM DMA, MBC1, MBC3.

ROM SPECIFICATIONS:

1. DMA_OAM_COPY (bench/roms/dma_oam_copy.asm):
   Tests: OAM DMA transfer correctness, CPU bus restriction during DMA
   Strategy: Write known sprite data to WRAM (0xC000-0xC09F), trigger DMA (write 0xC0 to 0xFF46), wait 160 M-cycles, verify OAM (0xFE00-0xFE9F) matches source. Test that CPU can only access HRAM during DMA.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_dma_trigger, __checkpoint_dma_complete, __checkpoint_oam_verify

2. MBC1_SWITCH (bench/roms/mbc1_switch.asm):
   Tests: MBC1 ROM bank switching (5-bit bank register, bank 0 maps to 1)
   Strategy: Write to 0x2000-0x3FFF to select ROM banks 1-31. Read known data from each bank (0x4000-0x7FFF) and verify. Test bank 0 -> 1 remapping.

3. MBC1_RAM (bench/roms/mbc1_ram.asm):
   Tests: MBC1 external RAM enable and bank select
   Strategy: Enable RAM (write 0x0A to 0x0000-0x1FFF), select RAM bank, write/read pattern to 0xA000-0xBFFF, verify. Test RAM disable.

4. MBC3_SWITCH (bench/roms/mbc3_switch.asm):
   Tests: MBC3 ROM bank switching (7-bit bank register)
   Strategy: Similar to MBC1_SWITCH but with larger bank space (0-127).

5. MBC3_RAM (bench/roms/mbc3_ram.asm):
   Tests: MBC3 external RAM and RTC register access
   Strategy: Enable RAM/RTC, select RAM banks 0-3, write/read patterns. Test RTC register access (if implemented).

ACCEPTANCE: All 5 ROMs assemble, pass ABI validation, registered in manifest.

title: Green CB_BITOPS custom ROM
description:
Write the CB_BITOPS custom test ROM and green it.

Test coverage:
- RLC/RRC/RL/RR for representative registers and values
- SLA/SRA/SRL for edge cases (MSB/LSB behavior)
- SWAP for various values
- BIT b,r for all bit positions (verify Z flag = !tested_bit, H=1, N=0)
- RES b,r and SET b,r (verify no flag changes, correct bit manipulation)
- Operations on (HL): read-modify-write with correct timing

Edge cases:
- Rotate through carry with carry=0 and carry=1
- SRA preserves MSB (arithmetic shift)
- SRL clears MSB (logical shift)
- SWAP of 0x00 sets Z flag

Oracle mode: instr_commit

title: Custom ROM checkpoint ABI specification
description:
Define the formal checkpoint ABI (§17.0.1) that ALL custom test ROMs must follow. This is the contract between ROM authors and the verification harness.

SPECIFICATION:

1. SYMBOL FILE (.sym):
   Every custom ROM MUST produce an RGBDS-compatible .sym file (via rgblink -n) containing at minimum:
   - __pass: executable label reached on successful test completion
   - __fail: executable label reached on test failure
   Both labels must be at executable code locations (not data) because PyBoy hooks work by replacing the instruction at the target address with a special opcode (§14.4).

2. OPTIONAL CHECKPOINT LABELS:
   - __checkpoint_<name>: intermediate verification points within a test
   - __commit_<name>: fine-grained hook-driven differential comparison points
   - __inject_begin_<name> / __inject_end_<name>: regions where external stimulus injection is valid
   These enable the harness to perform targeted M-cycle-level comparison without instrumenting every instruction.

3. WRAM SIGNATURE BLOCK:
   Every custom ROM should write a versioned signature block to a fixed WRAM region (suggested: 0xC000-0xC01F) containing:
   - Byte 0: ABI version (currently 0x01)
   - Byte 1: test result (0x00=running, 0x01=pass, 0xFF=fail)
   - Bytes 2-3: test case count (little-endian)
   - Bytes 4-5: pass count (little-endian)
   - Bytes 6-7: fail count (little-endian)
   - Bytes 8-15: reserved for debug counters
   - Bytes 16-31: ASCII test name (null-padded)
   This allows the harness to read test status from memory even if hooks are unavailable.

4. ROM STRUCTURE CONVENTIONS:
   - Entry point at 0x0150 (after Nintendo logo, using SkipBoot reset profile)
   - Header must pass rgbfix validation
   - ROM should begin with DI unless testing interrupt behavior
   - Tests should be self-contained: setup -> execute -> verify -> write result -> jump to __pass/__fail
   - No reliance on uninitialized memory values

5. MANIFEST INTEGRATION:
   Each ROM entry in bench/manifests/roms.yaml references oracle_mode, checkpoint_symbols, compare_scope, and memory_behavior_profile.

WHY THIS EXISTS: Without a formal ABI, ROM authors and harness maintainers make incompatible assumptions. The WRAM signature block provides a fallback verification path independent of PyBoy hooks. The __checkpoint_* and __commit_* labels enable surgical M-cycle comparison without full-trace overhead.

DELIVERABLES:
- spec/rom_abi.md: Formal specification document
- bench/tools/validate_rom_abi.py: Validation script for ROM+.sym pairs
- bench/roms/template.asm: Template ROM showing ABI in practice

ACCEPTANCE: validate_rom_abi.py passes on template ROM. ABI doc referenced by ROM build pipeline.

title: Background and window rendering
description:
Implement background and window pixel rendering.

Background rendering:
1. For each pixel on the current scanline:
2. Compute tilemap coordinates: ((SCY + LY) / 8, (SCX + pixel_x) / 8)
3. Look up tile index from selected tilemap (LCDC bit 3)
4. Fetch tile data from selected tile data area (LCDC bit 4)
5. Extract pixel from tile row using sub-tile coordinates
6. Apply BGP palette

Window rendering:
1. Window is active when LCDC bit 5 is set AND WY <= LY AND WX-7 <= pixel_x
2. Window uses its own internal line counter (doesn't use SCX/SCY)
3. Window tilemap selected by LCDC bit 6
4. Window overlays background (higher priority)

Tile data format:
- Each tile is 8x8 pixels, 2 bits per pixel
- 16 bytes per tile: 2 bytes per row, interleaved bit planes
- Byte 0 = low bits, Byte 1 = high bits for row 0, etc.

LCDC bit 4 tile data addressing:
- 1: unsigned, 0x8000 base (tiles 0-255)
- 0: signed, 0x9000 base (tiles -128 to 127, where tile 0 = 0x9000)

title: PPU rendering unit tests (tile fetch, sprites, palette)
description:
Create test/unit/test_ppu_render.py — isolated tests for PPU rendering logic.

BACKGROUND RENDERING TESTS:
1. Tile fetch: given LCDC settings and SCX/SCY, verify correct tile address computation
2. Tile data lookup: given tile index, verify correct tile data address (signed vs unsigned based on LCDC bit 4)
3. Palette application: given 2-bit pixel and BGP register, verify correct shade output
4. Scrolling: verify SCX/SCY offset wraps correctly (both within and across tile boundaries)
5. Tile map selection: verify LCDC bit 3 selects between 0x9800 and 0x9C00 tilemaps

WINDOW RENDERING TESTS:
6. Window activation: window appears when WX/WY conditions met AND LCDC bit 5 set
7. Window position: verify WX-7 offset and WY positioning
8. Window/BG priority: window overlaps background
9. Window internal line counter: window uses its own line counter, not LY

SPRITE RENDERING TESTS:
10. OAM scan: 10-sprite-per-line limit, verify correct sprite selection
11. Sprite X priority: leftmost sprite has priority (ties: lower OAM index wins)
12. Sprite Y positioning: sprite Y in OAM is Y+16 (offset by 16)
13. Sprite flipping: X-flip and Y-flip attributes
14. Sprite palette: OBP0 vs OBP1 selection
15. 8x16 sprite mode: LCDC bit 2, tile index bit 0 ignored
16. Sprite-to-BG priority: OAM attribute bit 7 (BG priority flag)

SEMANTIC VERIFICATION (§17.4):
The plan insists on SEMANTIC checks, not just frame hashes:
- "tilemap/window/sprite state queries"
- Verify the pixel pipeline produces correct intermediate values
- Frame hash is only the coarsest final checksum

LOGGING: Each test prints the rendering computation:
  [TILE] SCX=3 SCY=0 → tile map addr=0x9800 tile_x=0 tile_y=0 tile_idx=0x01
  [DATA] tile_idx=0x01 → data addr=0x8010 row=0 → lo=0x3C hi=0x7E
  [PIXEL] bit 7: (1,0)=0b01 → BGP shade 1 (light gray)

title: Phase machine for 16-bit arithmetic
description:
Implement phase machine for 16-bit arithmetic instructions.

Instructions:
- ADD HL,rr: 2 M-cycles (fetch + internal ALU with extra cycle for 16-bit add)
- INC rr: 2 M-cycles (fetch + internal)
- DEC rr: 2 M-cycles (fetch + internal)
- ADD SP,e8: 4 M-cycles (fetch + read imm + internal + internal)
- LD HL,SP+e8: 3 M-cycles (fetch + read imm + internal)

16-bit INC/DEC do NOT affect flags (unlike 8-bit INC/DEC).
ADD HL,rr affects N, H, C but preserves Z.
ADD SP,e8 and LD HL,SP+e8 have the tricky low-byte flag behavior (see ALU task).

title: IME state machine implementation
description:
Implement the IME (Interrupt Master Enable) state machine in the CPU.

States (§8.3):
  enum ImeState {
    Disabled,       // Interrupts disabled (after DI or reset)
    PendingEnable,  // EI executed, will enable AFTER next instruction
    Enabled,        // Interrupts enabled (can be serviced)
  }

Transitions:
- DI → ImeState::Disabled (immediate)
- EI → ImeState::PendingEnable (NOT immediate! Delayed by one instruction)
- After executing the instruction following EI: PendingEnable → Enabled
- Interrupt service entry → Disabled (interrupts disabled during service)
- RETI → Enabled (immediate, unlike EI)

The EI delay (§16.5 invariant): EI enables interrupts exactly one instruction later. This is critical for the common pattern:
  EI
  HALT  ; interrupts become enabled, then HALT waits for interrupt

If EI took effect immediately, the interrupt could fire before HALT executes.

The PendingEnable state is checked during the fetch phase: if an interrupt is pending AND IME is Enabled (not PendingEnable), service the interrupt instead of fetching the next instruction.

title: Formal verification: reset state correctness
description:
Create formal/cpu_reset.sby — proves that after reset, the CPU is in the exact expected state for the SkipBoot profile.

PROPERTY TO VERIFY:
After rst is asserted and then released, on the first m_ce cycle:
- PC = 0x0100 (SkipBoot entry point)
- SP = 0xFFFE
- A = 0x01, F = 0xB0 (Z=1, N=0, H=1, C=1 — DMG post-boot values)
- B=0x00, C=0x13, D=0x00, E=0xD8, H=0x01, L=0x4D (DMG post-boot values)
- IME = Disabled
- HaltState = Running
- Phase = Fetch
- BusReq = Read(0x0100) (fetching first instruction)

WHY FORMAL: Reset correctness is a boolean property over a small state space — perfect for BMC/induction. A simulation test checks one reset sequence; formal proves ALL possible reset sequences result in the correct state.

title: Bus observation sideband (BusObs) implementation in bus fabric
description:
Implement the simulation-only bus observation sideband (§12.2.1) in the bus fabric so CommitTrace records include memory region and access diagnostics.

BACKGROUND:
The CPU-facing bus contract (BusReq/BusResp from §12.2) is deliberately minimal--the CPU should not know whether 0xC000 is SPRAM, a Python array, or dual-port memory. But for VERIFICATION, we need to know which memory region was accessed and whether the access was blocked (e.g., during DMA or PPU pixel transfer). This information flows through BusObs, a simulation-only sideband that the bus fabric emits alongside the normal BusResp.

TYPES (already defined in bd-2on, implemented here):

  enum BusRegion { Rom, Vram, CartRam, Wram, Echo, Oam, NotUsable, Io, Hram, Ie }

  struct BusObs {
    region: BusRegion,   -- which address region was decoded
    blocked: bool,       -- was this access blocked by contention?
    owner: BusOwner,     -- who currently owns the bus?
  }

  enum BusOwner { Cpu, OamDma, Ppu, Idle }

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Extend the bus fabric (src/bus/membus.spade) to compute BusObs for every bus transaction
2. Address decode logic maps addr to BusRegion:
   - 0x0000-0x7FFF: Rom (or CartRam for MBC-mapped regions)
   - 0x8000-0x9FFF: Vram
   - 0xA000-0xBFFF: CartRam
   - 0xC000-0xDFFF: Wram
   - 0xE000-0xFDFF: Echo (mirrors Wram)
   - 0xFE00-0xFE9F: Oam
   - 0xFEA0-0xFEFF: NotUsable
   - 0xFF00-0xFF7F: Io
   - 0xFF80-0xFFFE: Hram
   - 0xFFFF: Ie
3. Blocked status is false for Wave A (no contention). Set true when DMA/PPU arbitration is added (Wave C/D).
4. BusOwner is Cpu for Wave A. Extended in Wave C (bd-1a6).

INTEGRATION WITH COMMIT TRACE:
- cpu_test_top and soc_lockstep_top expose BusObs alongside BusResp
- CommitTrace includes Optional<MemTouch> with region information
- The oracle comparator (bd-1au) can optionally compare BusRegion to detect address decode bugs

WHY THIS MATTERS:
- Catches address decode bugs that silently write to the wrong memory region
- Enables memory-region-aware coverage tracking (§16.3)
- Future DMA/PPU contention checking relies on this infrastructure
- Without BusObs, a mis-decoded address might pass all register comparisons but corrupt memory state

ACCEPTANCE: Bus fabric emits correct BusRegion for every address. Simulation tops expose BusObs. CommitTrace MemTouch includes region. At least one test validates address decode boundaries.

title: Timer/DIV entity (DIV, TIMA, TMA, TAC)
description:
Implement the timer subsystem in src/periph/timer.spade.

Registers:
- DIV (0xFF04): upper 8 bits of 16-bit system counter. Writing ANY value resets to 0.
- TIMA (0xFF05): timer counter. Increments at rate selected by TAC. Overflows → interrupt + reload from TMA.
- TMA (0xFF06): timer modulo. Value loaded into TIMA on overflow.
- TAC (0xFF07): timer control. Bit 2 = enable. Bits 1-0 = clock select:
  - 00: CPU clock / 1024 (4096 Hz)
  - 01: CPU clock / 16 (262144 Hz)
  - 10: CPU clock / 64 (65536 Hz)
  - 11: CPU clock / 256 (16384 Hz)

CRITICAL DESIGN DECISION (§6.1.1):
Timer derives from the SHARED timebase entity's sys_counter, NOT a private local counter. This prevents timer, CPU, and DMA blocks from drifting by one cycle.

Timer increment uses falling-edge detection on the selected bit of the system counter. This is the source of obscure timer bugs:
- Writing 0 to DIV can cause TIMA to increment if the selected counter bit was 1
- Changing TAC clock select can cause spurious TIMA increment
- These are real hardware behaviors that test ROMs verify

Timer overflow sequence:
1. TIMA overflows (0xFF → 0x00)
2. One M-cycle delay
3. TIMA reloaded from TMA
4. IF bit 2 (timer) set
5. During the delay cycle, writing TIMA cancels the reload and interrupt

title: Decoder: unprefixed opcodes
description:
Implement unprefixed opcode decoder in src/cpu/decode.spade.

Decoder function:
  fn decode(opcode: uint<8>) -> DecodedOp

Must cover all 256 unprefixed opcodes (0x00-0xFF):
- 8-bit loads: LD r,r / LD r,n / LD r,(HL) / LD (HL),r / LD (HL),n / LD A,(rr) / LD (rr),A
- 16-bit loads: LD rr,nn / LD SP,HL / LD (nn),SP / PUSH rr / POP rr / LD HL,SP+e8
- 8-bit ALU: ADD/ADC/SUB/SBC/AND/XOR/OR/CP with r/(HL)/n
- 16-bit arithmetic: ADD HL,rr / INC rr / DEC rr / ADD SP,e8
- 8-bit INC/DEC: INC r / DEC r / INC (HL) / DEC (HL)
- Rotate/shift (unprefixed): RLCA/RRCA/RLA/RRA
- Control flow: JP/JR/CALL/RET/RETI/RST (conditional and unconditional)
- Misc: NOP, HALT, STOP, DI, EI, DAA, CPL, SCF, CCF
- Invalid: 0xD3,0xDB,0xDD,0xE3,0xE4,0xEB,0xEC,0xED,0xF4,0xFC,0xFD

Drive the decode table from the canonical opcode metadata (spec/sm83_opcodes.py) to ensure completeness.

title: Decoder: CB-prefixed opcodes
description:
Implement CB-prefix decoder in src/cpu/decode.spade.

Function:
  fn decode_cb(opcode: uint<8>) -> DecodedOp

All 256 CB-prefixed opcodes follow a regular pattern:
- 0x00-0x07: RLC r (rotate left circular)
- 0x08-0x0F: RRC r (rotate right circular)
- 0x10-0x17: RL r (rotate left through carry)
- 0x18-0x1F: RR r (rotate right through carry)
- 0x20-0x27: SLA r (shift left arithmetic)
- 0x28-0x2F: SRA r (shift right arithmetic)
- 0x30-0x37: SWAP r (swap nibbles)
- 0x38-0x3F: SRL r (shift right logical)
- 0x40-0x7F: BIT b,r (test bit)
- 0x80-0xBF: RES b,r (reset bit)
- 0xC0-0xFF: SET b,r (set bit)

Register encoding in bits [2:0]: B,C,D,E,H,L,(HL),A
Bit index in bits [5:3] for BIT/RES/SET

The regular structure means decode_cb can be largely computed from bit slicing rather than a 256-entry lookup table.

title: ALU implementation: BIT/RES/SET (CB prefix)
description:
Implement CB-prefix bit operations in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- BIT b,r: test bit b of register r. Z=!bit_b, N=0, H=1, C=preserved
- RES b,r: reset (clear) bit b of register r. No flag changes.
- SET b,r: set bit b of register r. No flag changes.

b ranges from 0-7, covering all 8 bits.
Each operation exists for all 8 registers (B,C,D,E,H,L,(HL),A) = 8*8*3 = 192 CB opcodes total for these three families.

BIT is the only one that affects flags. RES and SET are pure data operations.

title: Fetch/execute phase machine for load instructions
description:
Implement the Phase state machine in src/cpu/control.spade and src/cpu/semantics.spade for load instruction families.

Load families to implement:
- LD r,r: single M-cycle, no memory access
- LD r,n: fetch immediate byte (ReadImm8 → LoadReg8 continuation)
- LD r,(HL): read from memory at HL (ReadMem → LoadReg8)
- LD (HL),r: write register to memory at HL (WriteMem → StoreDone)
- LD (HL),n: fetch immediate, then write to (HL) — two M-cycles after fetch
- LD A,(rr): read from BC/DE/HL address
- LD (rr),A: write A to BC/DE/HL address
- LD A,(nn): fetch 16-bit immediate, then read from that address
- LD (nn),A: fetch 16-bit immediate, then write A to that address
- LD rr,nn: fetch 16-bit immediate into register pair
- LD SP,HL: copy HL to SP (1 extra M-cycle)
- LD (nn),SP: store SP to memory at nn (16-bit immediate address)
- LD HL,SP+e8: SP + signed immediate, special flags (shares ALU with ADD SP,e8)
- LDH A,(n): read from 0xFF00+n
- LDH (n),A: write A to 0xFF00+n
- LDH A,(C): read from 0xFF00+C
- LDH (C),A: write A to 0xFF00+C
- LD A,(HL+): load A from (HL), then increment HL
- LD A,(HL-): load A from (HL), then decrement HL
- LD (HL+),A: store A to (HL), then increment HL
- LD (HL-),A: store A to (HL), then decrement HL
- PUSH rr: decrement SP, write high byte, decrement SP, write low byte
- POP rr: read low byte, increment SP, read high byte, increment SP

The phase machine uses typed continuations (§8.2). Each memory access transitions through ReadMem/WriteMem with the appropriate continuation carrying the semantic intent.

fn step_mcycle drives the transition: given current Phase + bus response → next Phase + bus request + delta.

title: ALU implementation: 16-bit ADD, ADD SP,e8, LD HL,SP+e8
description:
Implement 16-bit arithmetic in src/cpu/alu.spade.

Operations:
- ADD HL,rr: HL + rr, flags Z=PRESERVED, N=0, H=half-carry(bit 11), C=carry(bit 15)
- ADD SP,e8: SP + signed_extend(e8), flags Z=0, N=0, H=half-carry(bit 3!), C=carry(bit 7!)
- LD HL,SP+e8: same computation as ADD SP,e8, same flag behavior

TRICKY FLAG CASES (§11.3):
- ADD HL,rr: half-carry is from bit 11 (not bit 3), carry from bit 15 (not bit 7). Z flag is PRESERVED.
- ADD SP,e8: despite being 16-bit, H/C flags are computed from the LOW BYTE of SP + e8. Z is always 0. This is extremely counterintuitive.
- LD HL,SP+e8: SAME flag behavior as ADD SP,e8 (Z=0, N=0, H from low byte, C from low byte)

These are the most commonly mis-implemented ALU operations in Game Boy cores. Lift directly from gbxcule.

title: Metamorphic and invariant tests for CPU core
description:
Implement metamorphic and invariant tests from plan section 16.5 in test/unit/test_cpu_invariants.py.

These tests verify STRUCTURAL PROPERTIES of the CPU that must hold across ALL instructions, not just specific instruction correctness. They catch bugs that per-instruction tests miss: spurious register writes, timing violations, hold failures.

TESTS TO IMPLEMENT:

1. UNTOUCHED REGISTERS UNCHANGED:
   For each instruction, verify that registers NOT targeted by that instruction remain bit-identical after execution. Uses opcode metadata (bd-36u) to determine which registers each instruction writes.
   Example: LD B,0x42 must leave A, F, C, D, E, H, L, SP unchanged.

2. m_ce=0 HOLD (simulation complement to formal bd-1yk):
   Hold m_ce=0 for 100 clock cycles. Verify ALL architectural state (registers, PC, SP, IME, phase) unchanged.
   Verify no write-enable signals asserted.

3. BRANCH NOT-TAKEN CYCLE COUNT:
   For each conditional branch (JR cc, JP cc, CALL cc, RET cc), verify that when the condition is NOT met, the instruction takes the SHORT cycle count (from opcode metadata).

4. F LOW NIBBLE ALWAYS ZERO (simulation complement to formal bd-lfg):
   After every instruction execution, verify F & 0x0F == 0.
   Parameterized over all instruction families.

5. MEMORY WRITE TIMING:
   For instructions that write to memory, verify the write occurs only in the documented M-cycle (from opcode metadata). No early or late writes.

6. cpu_hold_only:
   Set cpu_hold_only=true in SimStimulus. Verify CPU state frozen but timer/peripheral time continues to advance.

7. freeze_arch_time:
   Set freeze_arch_time=true in SimStimulus. Verify both CPU and peripheral architectural time frozen.

8. HALT STILLNESS (when HALT implemented in Phase 4):
   HALT with no wake source: verify zero register writes, zero bus requests, zero ALU activity, stable phase/debug outputs. Tests plan section 18.5.

9. EI DELAY INVARIANT (when interrupts in Phase 4):
   EI enables interrupts exactly one instruction later. EI followed by DI leaves interrupts disabled.

10. INTERRUPT SERVICE SEQUENCE:
    When interrupt is acknowledged: push PC, jump to vector, clear IF bit. Verify exact M-cycle sequence.

PARAMETERIZATION:
- Tests 1, 3, 4, 5 are parameterized over instruction families using opcode metadata
- Test generates parameters dynamically from sm83_opcodes.py
- Missing opcode metadata coverage is flagged as a test warning

LOGGING FORMAT:
  [INVARIANT] untouched_registers
    [PARAM] instruction=LD_B_n8 (opcode=0x06)
    [CHECK] A unchanged: 0x00 == 0x00 OK
    [CHECK] F unchanged: 0xB0 == 0xB0 OK
    [CHECK] C unchanged: 0x13 == 0x13 OK
    ...
    [PASS] untouched_registers[LD_B_n8]

ACCEPTANCE: All invariants pass for all implemented instruction families. Coverage report shows which invariants are tested for which opcode families.

title: Author Wave B custom ROM assembly source
description:
Write SM83 assembly source for all 3 Wave B custom test ROMs (§17.2) targeting timer/interrupt correctness.

ROM SPECIFICATIONS:

1. TIMER_DIV_BASIC (bench/roms/timer_div_basic.asm):
   Tests: DIV free-running counter, DIV reset-on-write, TIMA increment rate, TAC prescaler selection, TMA reload on overflow
   Strategy:

   - Set TMA to known value, let TIMA overflow, verify TIMA reloads from TMA

   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_div_count, __checkpoint_div_reset, __checkpoint_tac_prescaler, __checkpoint_tma_reload

2. EI_DELAY (bench/roms/ei_delay.asm):

   - Test EI followed by: NOP, LD, ADD, JP, DI (verify DI after EI cancels)
   - Test EI followed by EI (verify no double-enable issues)
   - Test RETI (enables interrupts AND returns -- verify timing)

3. TIMER_IRQ_HALT (bench/roms/timer_irq_halt.asm):

   Strategy:
   - Configure timer for near-immediate overflow
   - Enable timer interrupt (IE bit 2)
   - EI / HALT
   - Verify: CPU halts, timer overflows, IF bit set, CPU wakes, vectors to 0x0050, ISR executes, RETI returns to instruction after HALT
   - Test with different wake sources (timer, serial, joypad via SimStimulus)
   - Test HALT without EI (HALT bug: CPU halts but wakes without servicing interrupt, PC fails to increment)
   Critical: HALT must produce real stillness (§18.5): no register writes, no bus requests, no ALU activity.
   Checkpoints: __checkpoint_halt_enter, __checkpoint_irq_fire, __checkpoint_isr_execute, __checkpoint_halt_wake

ACCEPTANCE: All 3 ROMs assemble, pass ABI validation, registered in manifest with oracle_mode=instr_commit.

title: RETI implementation
description:
Implement RETI instruction in the CPU phase machine.

RETI = RET + set IME to Enabled (immediately, unlike EI).

Sequence (4 M-cycles, same as RET):
1. Fetch RETI opcode
2. Pop low byte of return address from stack
3. Pop high byte of return address from stack
4. Internal cycle, set PC to popped address, set IME=Enabled

Unlike EI, RETI enables interrupts IMMEDIATELY (no one-instruction delay). This is because RETI is typically the last instruction of an interrupt handler, and the next instruction will be in the interrupted code which should already be interruptible.

RETI is the normal way to exit an interrupt handler. Using RET from an interrupt handler would leave IME=Disabled, causing interrupts to stop working.
close_reason: Already implemented and covered by test_semantics_flow
